Professor View 01 / 37
Business English -- 26 Aulas

Natalie Viegas

From an executive who survives in English to a leader who runs global meetings with authority and fluency

B1+ São Paulo, SP (from Rio) Account Executive / Microsoft Brasil 60 min / 2x semana / Online
Overall Progress0%
Speed:
Natalie Viegas
40 anos
Account Executive / Microsoft Brasil (Public Sector)
São Paulo, SP (natural do Rio de Janeiro)
B1+ (Intermediário Alto)
Business English -- Vendas Governo & Liderança
26 aulas de 60 min
Online (Zoom) / 2x por semana

Jornada de Transformação

De: Executiva que sobrevive em reuniões em inglês, traduzindo mentalmente do português e perdendo o timing das conversas com stakeholders globais

Para: Líder que conduz reuniões com VPs globais, negocia contratos, apresenta resultados e se posiciona com autoridade em inglês

Promessa do Programa

"De executiva que sobrevive em inglês a líder que conduz reuniões globais com autoridade e fluência"

Forças

  • Experiência sólida em vendas enterprise para setor público
  • Alta motivação: inglês é fator decisivo para promoção
  • Leitura de emails e documentos em inglês com boa compreensão
  • Vocabulário técnico de tecnologia acima da média para o nível
  • Perfil comunicativo: não tem medo de tentar falar

Pontos de Melhoria

  • Tradução mental do português para inglês em tempo real
  • Confusão entre Present Simple e Present Continuous
  • Pronúncia de clusters consonantais (stakeholder, procurement)
  • Vocabulário de negociação e argumentação ainda limitado
  • Hesitação em reuniões com VPs por insegurança linguística

Currículo -- 26 Aulas

#TemaFoco LinguísticoAtividade PrincipalHomework
1Who Is Natalie Viegas? -- Diagnostic + Executive Self-PortraitPresent Simple + Present ContinuousVP meeting role-playRecord 2-min self-introduction
2Your Monday at Microsoft -- Routines and Daily OperationsPresent Simple (routines) + Adverbs of frequencyDay-in-the-life presentationWrite daily routine paragraph
3Career Story -- From Rio to São PauloPast Simple + Time expressionsCareer timeline presentationRecord career story audio
4What Are You Working On? -- Current ProjectsPresent Continuous + Present PerfectProject update briefingWrite project status email
5The Art of Meetings -- Leading and ParticipatingModal verbs (should, could, would)Meeting simulationWrite meeting agenda
6Writing Business Reports -- Structure and ClarityPassive voice + Formal registerReport writing workshopDraft executive summary
7Handling Client Conversations -- Public Sector LanguageFirst conditional + Polite requestsClient call simulationRecord client follow-up
8Presenting to Leadership -- The Elevator PitchPresent Perfect + QuantifiersElevator pitch deliveryRefine pitch recording
9Agreeing and Disagreeing -- Professional DebatesOpinion frames + Hedging languageDebate simulationWrite opinion email
10Data and Numbers -- Presenting StatisticsComparatives + SuperlativesData presentationPrepare chart narration
11Email Excellence -- Writing Professional EmailsFormal vs informal registerEmail writing workshopWrite 3 business emails
12Midpoint Review -- Integration CheckAll structures (1-11)Full immersion simulationSelf-assessment recording
13Negotiation Basics -- Finding Common GroundSecond conditional + Negotiation framesNegotiation role-playWrite negotiation summary
14Technology Solutions -- Selling Software to GovernmentTechnical vocabulary + Explaining featuresProduct demo simulationRecord product pitch
15Cultural Intelligence -- Working with Global TeamsIndirect language + Diplomatic expressionsCross-cultural scenarioWrite multicultural email
16Strategic Planning -- Quarterly ReviewsFuture forms (will, going to, present continuous)QBR presentationDraft quarterly plan
17Conference Calls -- Virtual MeetingsPhrasal verbs for meetingsConference call simulationRecord meeting summary
18Problem-Solving -- When Things Go WrongPast Perfect + Cause and effectCrisis management role-playWrite incident report
19Feedback and Coaching -- Giving and ReceivingReported speech + Softening languageFeedback delivery practiceRecord feedback dialogue
20Networking -- Building Professional RelationshipsSmall talk + Follow-up languageNetworking event simulationWrite LinkedIn message
21Case Study -- A Real Microsoft ChallengeNarrative tenses integratedCase study analysisWrite case summary
22Presentation Skills -- Delivering with ImpactRhetorical devices + SignpostingFull presentation deliveryRefine presentation
23Writing for Impact -- Proposals and SummariesAdvanced connectors + CohesionProposal writing workshopDraft business proposal
24Leadership Communication -- Authority and EmpathyMixed conditionals + Empathetic languageLeadership scenarioRecord leadership speech
25Global VP Meeting -- Full Immersion SimulationAll structures integrated45-min immersion simulationFinal reflection audio
26Celebrating Transformation -- Final ReviewProgram review + Self-assessmentPortfolio presentationCelebration recording

Welcome, Natalie!

Onboarding

Welcome to your program! Before each class, complete the Pre-class activities. Here are 5 emergency phrases you can use at any time:

1 Could you repeat that, please?
2 I am not sure I understand. Could you explain?
3 Let me think about that for a moment.
4 That is a great question.
5 In my experience, I would say...
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Who Is Natalie Viegas?

Diagnostic + Executive Self-Portrait: Present Simple & Present Continuous
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Meet the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Oversee -- to watch over and manage a process or team
"I oversee public sector accounts across the southeast region."
Lead -- to be in charge of a team or project
"She leads the government sales team at Microsoft Brasil."
Handle -- to deal with or manage a situation or task
"We handle complex procurement processes for courts and ministries."
Liaise -- to communicate and cooperate between groups
"I liaise between the technical team and our government clients."
Report to -- to be accountable to a manager or superior
"I report to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America."
Be responsible for -- to have the duty of managing something
"I am responsible for closing enterprise deals with public institutions."
Stakeholder -- a person with interest or influence in a project
"Our key stakeholders include judges and public prosecutors."
Pipeline -- a set of potential deals or projects in progress
"We have a strong pipeline of government contracts this quarter."
Currently -- at the present time; now
"I am currently working on a major deal with the Ministry of Justice."
Drive -- to push forward or cause progress
"Our team drives digital transformation in the public sector."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its English definition.

Oversee
Lead
Handle
Liaise
Report to
Be responsible for
Stakeholder
Pipeline
Currently
Drive

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Natalie is an Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil. She works in the public sector team. Every day, she oversees government accounts and handles deals with courts and public ministries. She reports to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America. She liaises between the technical team and government clients regularly. Right now, she is working on a major deal with the Ministry of Justice. Her team is driving a digital transformation project. She is currently leading the proposal phase. Natalie drives results and is responsible for closing enterprise deals across the southeast region.

1. Which sentence describes what Natalie does every day (routine)?
A She is working on a major deal.
B She oversees government accounts and handles deals with courts.
C She is currently leading the proposal phase.
2. Which sentence describes something happening right now (temporary/current)?
A She reports to the VP of Public Sector Sales.
B She liaises between the technical team and clients.
C Her team is driving a digital transformation project.
3. "Natalie drives results." Which tense is this?
A Present Simple (general fact about her role).
B Present Continuous (happening now).
C Past Simple (completed action).

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple = facts, habits, routines (things that happen regularly or are permanent truths)
Present Continuous = actions happening now, temporary situations, current projects (actions in progress or temporary situations)

TenseStructureExample
Present Simple (+)Subject + verb (base/s)"She oversees public sector accounts."
Present Simple (-)Subject + do/does not + verb"She does not handle private sector deals."
Present Simple (?)Do/Does + subject + verb?"Does she report to the VP?"
Present Continuous (+)Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing"She is driving a transformation project."
Present Continuous (-)Subject + am/is/are + not + verb-ing"She is not working on that project anymore."

Common Mistake:

"I am work at Microsoft."

"I work at Microsoft." (permanent fact = Present Simple)

Use Present Simple for permanent facts. Use Present Continuous (am/is/are + -ing) only for actions in progress or temporary situations.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Made a mistake? Read the hint carefully and try again.

"I the pipeline for public sector accounts in Brazil."
"She client meetings with government stakeholders."
"I all communications with the procurement department."
"I with legal teams to ensure contract compliance."
"She the regional VP every Monday."
"I am leading a digital transformation project."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I oversee public sector accounts, working with courts and ministries."
? "Good morning, everyone. I am Natalie Viegas."
? "I would love to discuss how our teams can collaborate."
? "I am currently driving a digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice."
? "I am an Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I am an Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil.
I am currently driving a digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice.
I have been working in public sector sales for over ten years.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are at a Microsoft global meeting. A VP asks: "What do you do?" What is the best response?
A "I am Natalie. I do sales in Brazil."
B "I am an Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil. I oversee public sector accounts for courts and ministries."
C "I have a job at Microsoft in São Paulo."
2. A colleague asks: "What are you working on right now?" What is the correct answer?
A "I work on a project with the Ministry of Justice."
B "I am currently driving a digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice."
C "I am drive a project now."
3. Someone asks: "Who do you report to?" What is the correct response?
A "I report to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America."
B "I am reporting to the VP since last year."
C "My report is the VP."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Imagine you are at a Microsoft global summit. A VP from Asia Pacific asks: "Tell me about yourself and what you are working on." Record your answer in English. Try to include: your name, your role, what you currently oversee, and one current project.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1 Let me introduce myself. I am Natalie Viegas from Microsoft Brasil.
2 I oversee public sector sales for courts and ministries.
3 I am currently working on a cloud solution for the Ministry of Justice.
4 I report to the VP of Public Sector for Latin America.
5 I would love to discuss how our teams can collaborate.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

Your Monday at Microsoft

Routines and Daily Operations: Present Simple & Adverbs of Frequency
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Meet the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Schedule -- a plan that lists times for events or activities
"I check my schedule every morning before anything else."
Commute -- to travel regularly between home and work
"I commute from Vila Madalena to the Microsoft office in Itaim Bibi."
Attend -- to be present at an event or meeting
"I attend at least three client meetings every week."
Brief -- to give someone the key information about something
"I brief my team on the pipeline status every Monday."
Review -- to examine or look at something carefully
"I always review the contract details before a client call."
Prioritize -- to decide which tasks are the most important
"I prioritize urgent client requests over internal emails."
Follow up -- to take further action after initial contact
"I need to follow up with the Ministry of Justice on the proposal."
Deadline -- the latest time by which something must be done
"The deadline for the quarterly report is next Friday."
Catch up -- to spend time finding out what has happened
"I usually catch up with my team at nine in the morning."
Wrap up -- to finish or complete something
"Let me wrap up this presentation before the next meeting."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its English definition.

Schedule
Commute
Attend
Brief
Review
Prioritize
Follow up
Deadline
Catch up
Wrap up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Natalie always starts her Monday early. She commutes from Vila Madalena to the Microsoft office in Itaim Bibi. When she arrives, she usually checks her schedule and reviews urgent emails. She always attends the leadership meeting at nine. After that, she briefs her team on the week's priorities. She often needs to follow up with stakeholders before a deadline. She rarely skips her morning routine. In the afternoon, Natalie usually catches up with her sales team. She never leaves the office without wrapping up her reports. She sometimes prioritizes internal meetings over emails, but she always puts clients first.

1. What does Natalie always do when she arrives at the office?
A She briefs her team on the pipeline.
B She checks her schedule and reviews urgent emails.
C She catches up with the sales team.
2. How often does Natalie skip her morning routine?
A She sometimes skips it.
B She never skips it.
C She rarely skips it.
3. Where is the adverb "usually" placed in "She usually checks her schedule"?
A Before the main verb (checks).
B After the main verb.
C At the end of the sentence.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Simple for Routines = actions that happen regularly, habits, general facts
Adverbs of Frequency = words that indicate HOW OFTEN something happens

AdverbFrequencyExample
always100%"I always check my schedule."
usually80%"I usually attend the 9 AM meeting."
often60%"She often briefs her team."
sometimes40%"He sometimes works late."
rarely10%"She rarely skips meetings."
never0%"I never leave without wrapping up."

Position Rule:

Before the main verb: "I always review my emails."

After the verb BE: "She is usually on time."

Before the main verb. After the verb BE (am/is/are).

Common Mistake:

"I check always my email."

"I always check my email."

The frequency adverb comes BEFORE the main verb, never after.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Made a mistake? Read the hint carefully and try again.

"I always my schedule before the first meeting."
"She usually by car during rush hour."
"I need to with the client before the deadline."
"He never the Monday briefing."
"We always client requests over internal tasks."
"Let me the report before the deadline."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I attend the leadership meeting at nine."
? "I wrap up my reports before leaving the office."
? "I commute from Vila Madalena to the office."
? "I brief my team and follow up with stakeholders."
? "I check my schedule and review urgent emails."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I check my schedule every morning and prioritize my tasks for the day.
I usually catch up with my team before I attend any client meetings.
I always review the contract details and brief my team before the deadline.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Your manager asks: "How do you start your Mondays?" What is the best response?
A "I am checking my schedule now."
B "I always check my schedule and review urgent emails first."
C "I check always my emails."
2. A colleague asks: "Do you usually attend the 9 AM meeting?" You go most weeks. What do you say?
A "Yes, I usually attend the nine AM meeting."
B "Yes, I am attending usually the meeting."
C "Yes, I attend always the meeting."
3. Someone asks: "What do you do before you leave the office?" What is the correct answer?
A "I am wrapping up my reports every day."
B "I wrap up always my reports."
C "I always wrap up my reports and prioritize emails."

Stage 5: Think About It

Reflection
Describe your typical Monday at work. Include: what time you commute, what you do first, who you meet with, and how you wrap up your day. Try to use at least 5 vocabulary words and 3 adverbs of frequency.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1 I always check my schedule first thing in the morning.
2 I need to follow up with the client by Friday.
3 Let me brief the team on the pipeline status.
4 I usually catch up with my team at nine AM.
5 The deadline for this proposal is next Wednesday.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

Career Story -- From Rio to São Paulo

Past Simple (regular + irregular) & Time Expressions
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Meet the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Relocate -- to move to a new place, especially for work
"I relocated from Rio de Janeiro to São Paulo four years ago."
Transition -- to change from one job, role, or situation to another
"I transitioned from a different industry into technology sales."
Pursue -- to try to achieve a goal or follow a career path
"I pursued a career in enterprise sales because I love working with clients."
Gain (experience) -- to get or acquire something valuable over time
"I gained valuable experience working with public institutions."
Accomplish -- to succeed in doing something important
"We accomplished our revenue target three months ahead of schedule."
Challenge -- a difficult task or situation that tests your abilities
"The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city and a new industry at the same time."
Opportunity -- a chance to do something new or better
"Microsoft gave me the opportunity to work with government clients across Brazil."
Previous -- coming before in time or order
"In my previous role, I managed accounts in the private sector."
Achievement -- something important that you have done successfully
"My greatest achievement last year was closing a multi-million-dollar deal."
Milestone -- an important event or turning point in development
"Moving to São Paulo was a major milestone in my career."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its English definition.

Relocate
Transition
Pursue
Gain
Accomplish
Challenge
Opportunity
Previous
Achievement
Milestone

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Four years ago, Natalie relocated from Rio de Janeiro to São Paulo. She transitioned from a different industry into technology sales. She pursued a career at Microsoft because she wanted to work with bigger clients. In her previous role, she managed accounts in the private sector. She gained valuable experience working with public institutions. The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city and a new industry at the same time. Last year, she accomplished her revenue target three months ahead of schedule. Microsoft gave her the opportunity to work with government clients across Brazil. That was a major milestone in her career. She did not expect to grow so fast, but every step led her to where she is today.

1. "Natalie relocated from Rio four years ago." What tense is this?
A Past Simple (completed action in the past).
B Present Perfect (action connected to now).
C Present Simple (habitual action).
2. Which time expression tells us WHEN something happened?
A "usually" (frequency)
B "four years ago" (specific past time)
C "currently" (present time)
3. "Microsoft gave her the opportunity." What is the base form of "gave"?
A gaved
B give
C giving

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Past Simple = completed actions in the past with a specific time reference (completed actions in the past with a specific time reference)

TypeStructureExample
Regular (+)Subject + verb-ed"I relocated to São Paulo."
Irregular (+)Subject + irregular past form"Microsoft gave me the opportunity."
Negative (-)Subject + did not + base verb"I did not expect to grow so fast."
Question (?)Did + subject + base verb?"Did you work in Rio before?"

Common irregular verbs: was/were, had, went, came, took, made, got, left, gave, led

Time expressions (time expressions): ago, last year, in 2022, when I was..., before that, after that, then, at that time

Common Mistake:

"I relocate to SP four years ago."

"I relocated to SP four years ago." (past action = Past Simple with -ed)

Use Past Simple (verb-ed or irregular form) when the action happened at a specific time in the past. The time marker "ago" requires Past Simple.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Made a mistake? Read the hint and try again.

"I to São Paulo and pursued a career in tech sales."
"She experience in three different industries before joining Microsoft."
"The biggest was learning a completely new market."
"He all his targets in the first quarter."
"My role gave me the opportunity to travel across Latin America."
"That was a major milestone for the entire team."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "I gained valuable experience working with public institutions."
? "In my previous role, I worked in a completely different industry."
? "My greatest achievement was closing a multi-million-dollar deal."
? "Microsoft gave me the opportunity to work with government clients."
? "I relocated from Rio to São Paulo and pursued a career in tech."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago and transitioned into technology sales.
In my previous role, I gained valuable experience working with government clients.
My greatest achievement was closing a multi-million-dollar deal ahead of schedule.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
A new colleague asks about your career path. How do you respond?
A "I am relocating from Rio to São Paulo."
B "I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago."
C "I relocate from Rio to São Paulo four years ago."
Your VP asks: "What did you do before Microsoft?" Best answer:
A "I am working in a different industry before."
B "I work in the private sector for many years."
C "In my previous role, I managed accounts in the private sector."
How do you form a question about someone's past career?
A "Where you worked before Microsoft?"
B "Where did you work before Microsoft?"
C "Where did you worked before Microsoft?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are at a Microsoft networking event. A new colleague asks: "Tell me about your career journey. How did you get here?" Record a 1-minute answer using Past Simple and time expressions (ago, last year, before that, then). Try to use at least 5 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1 I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago.
2 In my previous role, I worked in a completely different industry.
3 I gained valuable experience working with government clients.
4 The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city and a new job.
5 Microsoft gave me the opportunity to grow as a professional.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

What Are You Working On?

Present Continuous + Present Perfect & Project Briefing
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Meet the key words for this lesson. Click Listen to hear the pronunciation.

Proposal -- a formal document presenting a plan for approval
"We submitted a proposal to the Ministry of Justice last month."
Scope -- the range or extent of a project's objectives
"The scope of this project includes cloud migration and data security."
Deliverable -- a specific output or result that must be completed by a deadline
"The first deliverable is due at the end of this sprint."
Allocate -- to set aside money, time, or resources for a specific purpose
"I have allocated the budget for the next quarter."
Streamline -- to make a process more efficient by removing unnecessary steps
"We need to streamline the procurement process to save time."
Roll out -- to introduce or launch a product, service, or system gradually
"We are rolling out the new case management system this month."
Assess -- to evaluate the quality, importance, or progress of something
"I am assessing the risks before the next sprint begins."
Sprint -- a short, focused period of intensive work in project management
"The team has completed three sprints so far."
Track -- to follow and monitor the progress of a project or task
"I track all the deliverables in our project dashboard."
Scalable -- designed to grow or expand without losing performance or quality
"The system needs to be scalable for other government agencies."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its English definition.

Proposal
Scope
Deliverable
Allocate
Streamline
Roll out
Assess
Sprint
Track
Scalable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Natalie is currently working on a major project for the Ministry of Justice. She is rolling out a new cloud-based case management system. The team has already completed three sprints, and they have delivered the first set of features. She has allocated the budget for the next quarter, and she is tracking the project deliverables weekly. The proposal has been approved, and the scope is clearly defined. Natalie is assessing the risks before the next sprint, and she has streamlined the approval process to save time. The system needs to be scalable because more government agencies have expressed interest. She has not finished the compliance review yet, but the team is working on it right now.

1. "Natalie is currently working on a major project." What tense is this?
A Present Continuous (action happening right now).
B Present Perfect (result connected to now).
C Past Simple (completed action).
2. "The team has completed three sprints." Why is Present Perfect used here?
A The action is happening right now.
B The action started in the past and the result matters now.
C The action happened at a specific time in the past.
3. Which sentence describes an action IN PROGRESS right now?
A "She has allocated the budget."
B "She is assessing the risks."
C "She allocated the budget last week."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Present Continuous = actions happening RIGHT NOW or currently in progress

TypeStructureExample
Affirmative (+)Subject + am/is/are + verb-ing"I am working on a proposal."
Negative (-)Subject + am/is/are + not + verb-ing"We are not tracking that yet."
Question (?)Am/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?"Are you rolling out the new system?"

Present Perfect = actions that started in the past with results or relevance NOW

TypeStructureExample
Affirmative (+)Subject + have/has + past participle"I have allocated the budget."
Negative (-)Subject + have/has + not + past participle"We have not finished the review."
Question (?)Have/Has + subject + past participle?"Have you completed the sprint?"

Signal words: Present Continuous = now, currently, right now, at the moment, this week. Present Perfect = already, yet, so far, just, ever, never, recently.

Common Mistake:

"I am work on a new proposal right now."

"I am working on a new proposal right now." (Present Continuous = am/is/are + verb-ING)

Present Continuous requires the -ing form. "Am work" is incomplete. Use "am working."

Common Mistake:

"We have complete three sprints so far."

"We have completed three sprints so far." (Present Perfect = have/has + PAST PARTICIPLE)

Present Perfect requires the past participle form. "Have complete" is wrong. Use "have completed."

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blanks

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Made a mistake? Read the hint and try again.

"I am currently on a new proposal for the government."
"We have already the first three sprints."
"She is out the new system this quarter."
"They have the budget for the next phase."
"I have the approval process to save time."
"The team is all the deliverables in the dashboard."

Stage 2: Put in Order

Order

Put the sentences in the correct order.

? "The team has completed three sprints so far."
? "Two more agencies have expressed interest in the system."
? "We received the proposal from the Ministry of Justice."
? "We are currently rolling out the first module."
? "I assessed the scope and allocated the budget."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, record, and compare your pronunciation.

I am currently working on a cloud migration project for the Ministry of Justice.
We have completed three sprints so far and delivered the core features.
The system is scalable, and two other agencies have already expressed interest.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
Your VP asks: "What are you doing right now?" Best answer:
A "I work on the proposal."
B "I am working on the cloud migration proposal."
C "I have worked on the proposal."
A colleague asks: "How many sprints have you finished?" Best answer:
A "We are completing three sprints."
B "We completed three sprints yesterday."
C "We have completed three sprints so far."
How do you describe something that started in the past and has results NOW?
A Present Continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing)
B Present Perfect (have/has + past participle)
C Past Simple (verb-ed)

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine your VP asks: "What projects are you working on, and what have you accomplished so far?" Record a 1-minute project briefing using Present Continuous for current activities and Present Perfect for results. Try to use at least 5 vocabulary words from this lesson.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1 I am currently working on a major project for the Ministry of Justice.
2 We have completed three sprints so far.
3 I have allocated the budget for next quarter.
4 The team is tracking all the deliverables.
5 The system has been designed to be scalable.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

The Art of Meetings -- Leading and Participating

Master meeting vocabulary and modal verbs (should, could, would) to lead and participate in professional meetings with confidence.
▼

Stage 1.1: Key Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Agenda.A list of topics to discuss in a meeting
"Let me share the agenda before we begin."
Recap.To summarize the main points of a discussion
"Let me recap the main points before we close."
Action item.A specific task assigned to someone after a meeting
"We have three action items from today's meeting."
Consensus.General agreement among a group of people
"We should reach a consensus before making a decision."
Facilitate.To guide or manage a discussion or process
"She facilitates our weekly strategy sessions."
Objective.The goal or purpose of a meeting or project
"The objective of this meeting is to review our Q3 targets."
Minutes.The official written record of what was discussed in a meeting
"Could you send the minutes to the team after the meeting?"
Chair.To lead or preside over a meeting
"I will chair the quarterly review next Monday."
Adjourn.To formally end a meeting or session
"If there are no other questions, we should adjourn."
Clarify.To make something easier to understand by explaining it more clearly
"Could you clarify that point?"

Stage 1.2: Match the Definitions

Practice
Agenda
Recap
Action item
Consensus
Facilitate
Objective
Minutes
Chair
Adjourn
Clarify

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Natalie is preparing for her weekly team meeting at Microsoft. She should review the agenda before the meeting starts. She could ask David to share the latest numbers. She would like to recap the action items from last week. The team should try to reach a consensus on the budget before they adjourn. Natalie would prefer to chair the meeting herself so she can facilitate the discussion on Q3 targets. David could send the minutes to the team after the meeting. The main objective is to clarify the next steps for the Ministry of Justice proposal.

1. What should Natalie do before the meeting starts?
A Review the agenda
B Send the minutes
C Adjourn the meeting
2. What would Natalie like to do with last week's action items?
A Delete them
B Recap them
C Ignore them
3. What is the main objective of the meeting?
A To celebrate the team's success
B To hire new employees
C To clarify the next steps for the Ministry proposal

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Modal Verbs: should, could, would

Modal verbs are used before the BASE FORM of a verb (no -s, no -ing, no "to"). The only exception is would like to.
Os modais vem antes do verbo na forma BASE (sem -s, sem -ing, sem "to"). A unica excecao e would like to.

ModalUse / UsoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
shouldAdvice, recommendation
Conselho, recomendacao		should + base verb
should + verbo base		We should review the agenda.
Deveriamos revisar a pauta.
couldPossibility, polite request
Possibilidade, pedido educado		could + base verb
could + verbo base		Could you send the minutes?
Voce poderia enviar a ata?
wouldPolite preference, hypothetical
Preferencia educada, hipotese		would + base verb
would like to + verb
would + verbo base		I would like to recap the items.
Eu gostaria de recapitular os itens.

Common mistakes / Erros comuns: "She should reviews" (WRONG -- no -s after modal), "Could you sharing" (WRONG -- base form, not -ing), "I should to review" (WRONG -- no "to" after should/could).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"We review the agenda before starting the meeting."
" you send the minutes to the team by Friday?"
"I like to recap the main action items."
"She facilitate the next quarterly review."
"We adjourn the meeting if there are no more questions."
" you mind chairing the meeting on Monday?"

Stage 2: Meeting Steps -- Put in Order

Order

Listen to the audio, then arrange the meeting steps in the correct order.

? Discuss each item on the agenda
? Share the agenda at the beginning
? Adjourn the meeting
? State the objective of the meeting
? Recap action items and assign tasks

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
We should review the agenda before we start. Could you share the screen?
I would like to recap the action items from today's meeting.
Would you mind sending the minutes? We should follow up by Friday.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. You are starting a meeting and want to tell the team what topics you will cover. You say:
A "Let me share the agenda for today's meeting."
B "I would like to adjourn this meeting."
C "Could you send the minutes?"
2. You want to politely suggest that the team discuss the budget first. You say:
A "We must discuss the budget now."
B "We should discuss the budget first."
C "I am discussing the budget."
3. The meeting is over and there are no more questions. You say:
A "Let me share the agenda."
B "We should reach a consensus."
C "If there are no other questions, we could adjourn."
4. You want to politely ask a colleague to lead the next meeting. You say:
A "You chair the meeting."
B "Would you mind chairing the next meeting?"
C "I should facilitate the session."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are chairing a meeting at Microsoft. You need to: share the agenda, discuss the Q3 targets, assign action items, and close the meeting. Record yourself leading this meeting using should, could, and would.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1 Let me share the agenda for today's meeting.
2 We should move to the next item.
3 I would like to recap the action items.
4 Could you clarify that point?
5 If there are no other questions, let us adjourn.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

Writing Business Reports -- Passive Voice in Action

Master report vocabulary and passive voice (was drafted, have been made, will be implemented) to present business reports with clarity and professionalism.
▼

Stage 1.1: Key Vocabulary

Vocabulary
Draft.To write the first version of a document
"The report was drafted last week."
Revise.To review and improve a document or plan
"The report was drafted and revised by the legal team."
Outline.A structured plan or summary of main points
"An outline was prepared before drafting the full report."
Findings.The results or conclusions discovered during research or analysis
"The findings were presented to the board on Monday."
Recommendation.A formal suggestion about the best course of action
"Two recommendations have been made for Q4."
Executive summary.A brief overview at the beginning of a report covering key points
"The executive summary must be concise and clear."
Implement.To put a plan or decision into action
"The changes will be implemented in Q4."
Comply.To follow rules, regulations, or standards
"All reports must comply with industry benchmarks."
Benchmark.A standard used to measure or compare performance
"Compliance benchmarks need to be updated."
Concise.Brief and clear, without unnecessary detail
"The executive summary must be concise."

Stage 1.2: Match the Definitions

Practice
Draft
Revise
Outline
Findings
Recommendation
Executive summary
Implement
Comply
Benchmark
Concise

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

Grammar

Natalie is preparing the quarterly report for Microsoft. The outline was drafted by her team last Monday. The findings were presented to the compliance board on Tuesday. Two recommendations have been made: first, the onboarding process should be streamlined; second, the compliance benchmarks need to be updated. The executive summary was revised and approved by legal. The report must comply with industry standards and be concise. The changes will be implemented in Q4. A timeline has been drafted and shared with all stakeholders.

1. When was the outline drafted?
A Last Friday
B Last Monday
C This morning
2. How many recommendations have been made?
A One
B Two
C Three
3. When will the changes be implemented?
A Next year
B Next week
C In Q4

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip

Grammar

Passive Voice in Business Writing

The passive voice is used when the ACTION is more important than WHO did it. It is very common in business reports, legal documents, and formal writing.
A voz passiva e usada quando a ACAO e mais importante do que quem a realizou. E muito comum em relatorios, documentos legais e escrita formal.

Tense / TempoStructure / EstruturaExample / Exemplo
Past Simple Passive
Passado Simples Passivo		was/were + past participle
was/were + particípio		"The report was drafted."
O relatório foi redigido.
Present Perfect Passive
Presente Perfeito Passivo		has/have been + past participle
has/have been + particípio		"Recommendations have been made."
Recomendações foram feitas.
Future Passive
Futuro Passivo		will be + past participle
will be + particípio		"Changes will be implemented."
As mudanças serão implementadas.

Common mistakes / Erros comuns: "The report draft by the team." (WRONG -- needs was), "The findings was presented." (WRONG -- use were for plural), "The changes have implement." (WRONG -- needs been implemented).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice
"The report drafted last week."
"The findings presented to the board."
"Two recommendations been made."
"The executive summary reviewed by legal."
"Reports submitted every quarter."
"The changes be implemented in Q4."

Stage 2: Report Writing Steps -- Put in Order

Order

Listen to the audio, then arrange the report writing steps in the correct order.

? Draft the report
? Create an outline
? Implement recommendations
? Revise and submit the executive summary
? Research and gather findings

Stage 3: Pronunciation Practice

Speaking
The report was drafted last week. It has been revised by the legal team.
The findings were presented to the board. Two recommendations have been made.
The executive summary was reviewed and approved. It is concise and clear.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
1. Your VP asks: "Was the report finished by the team?" You answer using passive voice:
A "The team was drafted the report."
B "Yes, the report was drafted and submitted on time."
C "Yes, they draft it."
2. You need to say that the board received the results of your analysis. You say:
A "The board was presented."
B "The findings were presented to the board."
C "The findings have present to the board."
3. Your colleague asks about the formal suggestions in the report. You say:
A "Two recommendations are make."
B "Two recommendations have been made for Q4."
C "Two recommendations was made."
4. Your VP asks when the new process will start. You say:
A "The changes will implement in Q4."
B "The changes will be implemented before the deadline."
C "The changes been implemented."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection
Imagine you are presenting a quarterly report at Microsoft to leadership. The report was drafted by your team and revised by legal. The findings were presented last week. Record yourself presenting the key points using passive voice.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1 The report was drafted and submitted on time.
2 The findings were presented to the leadership team.
3 The executive summary must be concise.
4 Two recommendations have been made for Q4.
5 The changes will be implemented before the deadline.
Aula 7 -- Pre-class

Procurement & Tenders -- First Conditional and Polite Requests

Talk about how a tender works and what happens next, using the first conditional (If + present, will + verb) and polite requests (Could you...? / Would you mind...?). New vocabulary: procurement, tender, bid, vendor, clause, compliance, approval, negotiate, quotation, shortlist.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Procurement -- the process of buying goods and services for a company
"Our procurement team buys software for the government." (Compras / aquisições)
Tender -- a formal process where companies offer to do work at a price
"The ministry opened a tender for cloud services." (Licitação)
Bid -- a formal offer to win a contract at a certain price
"We submitted our bid before the deadline." (Proposta)
Vendor -- a company that sells goods or services to a client
"Microsoft is a vendor for many public agencies." (Fornecedor)
Clause -- one specific condition or part of a contract
"The contract has a clause about data security." (Cláusula)
Compliance -- following the official rules and laws
"Public contracts require strict compliance." (Conformidade)
Approval -- official permission to go ahead with something
"We are waiting for the manager's approval." (Aprovação)
Negotiate -- to discuss terms in order to reach an agreement
"We negotiate the price and the timeline." (Negociar)
Quotation -- a written document with the prices for a job
"Please send us a quotation by Friday." (Orçamento)
Shortlist -- to choose the best options from a larger group
"The client shortlisted three vendors." (Pré-selecionar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Procurement
Tender
Bid
Vendor
Clause
Compliance
Approval
Negotiate
Quotation
Shortlist

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie is preparing a bid for a government tender. She tells her team the plan. "If we submit the bid on time, the client will review it on Monday. If our compliance documents are complete, the bid will be valid." She also needs help from the finance team, so she sends a polite message: "Could you send me the quotation today? And would you mind checking the new clause about data security?" Natalie knows that if the client shortlists her company, they will negotiate the final price next week.

1. What will happen if the team submits the bid on time?
A The client will review it on Monday.
B The bid will not be valid.
C The team will lose the tender.
2. Which message is a polite request?
A "Send me the quotation now."
B "Could you send me the quotation today?"
C "I need the quotation."
3. What will the two sides do if the client shortlists the company?
A They will negotiate the final price next week.
B They will cancel the contract.
C They will open a new tender.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- First Conditional and Polite Requests

GRAMMAR

Usamos o first conditional para falar de um resultado provável no futuro, e pedidos educados para soar profissional. / We use the first conditional for a likely future result, and polite requests to sound professional.

FormUseExample
If + present, will + verba likely future resultIf we submit on time, the client will review it.
will + verb, if + presentsame idea, other orderThe bid will be valid if the forms are complete.
Could you + verb?a polite requestCould you send the quotation?
Would you mind + verb-ing?a very polite requestWould you mind clarifying the clause?

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de if use o presente, não o futuro: "If we submit..." (não "If we will submit..."). O will fica na outra parte da frase. Para pedidos, use Could you + verbo no infinitivo, e Would you mind + verbo com -ing ("Would you mind sending...?", não "Would you mind to send...?").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If we submit the bid on time, the client it."
"The bid will be valid if the compliance forms complete."
" send me the quotation today?"
"Would you mind the new clause?"
"If the client shortlists us, we the price."
"We cannot sign if the vendor does not accept this ."

Stage 2: Put the Process in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a tender in the correct order.

?The client checks compliance and shortlists the best vendors.
?The client opens a tender for the new project.
?The client gives approval and both sides sign the contract.
?Several vendors prepare their bids and send a quotation.
?The two sides negotiate the price and the main clauses.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

If we submit the bid on time, the client will review it.
Se enviarmos a proposta no prazo, o cliente vai analisá-la.
Could you send me the quotation today?
Você poderia me enviar o orçamento hoje?
Would you mind clarifying the new clause?
Você se importaria de esclarecer a nova cláusula?
If the client shortlists us, we will negotiate the price.
Se o cliente nos pré-selecionar, vamos negociar o preço.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to talk about a likely future result. Which is correct?
A "If we will win the tender, we sign next week."
B "If we win the tender, we will sign next week."
C "If we win the tender, we sign will next week."
You ask a colleague for help in a very polite way. Which is correct?
A "Would you mind sending the compliance forms?"
B "Would you mind to send the compliance forms?"
C "Would you mind send the compliance forms?"
A supplier offers a price to win the contract. What do they send?
A a bid
B a clause
C an approval

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think about a real deal or project at Microsoft. Describe the next steps using the first conditional ("If we..., we will...") and make two polite requests to your team ("Could you...?" / "Would you mind...?"). Use new words like tender, bid, vendor, compliance, clause, negotiate and approval.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1If we submit the bid on time, the client will review it.Se enviarmos a proposta no prazo, o cliente vai analisá-la.
2Could you send me the quotation today?Você poderia me enviar o orçamento hoje?
3Would you mind clarifying the new clause?Você se importaria de esclarecer a nova cláusula?
4We need to negotiate the price and the compliance terms.Precisamos negociar o preço e os termos de conformidade.
5If the client shortlists us, we will negotiate next week.Se o cliente nos pré-selecionar, vamos negociar na próxima semana.
Aula 8 -- Pre-class

Presenting to Leadership -- Present Perfect and Quantifiers

Deliver a short elevator pitch about your results, using the present perfect (have/has + past participle) and quantifiers (many, several, a lot of, plenty of). New vocabulary: pitch, value proposition, highlight, audience, takeaway, impact, persuade, outcome, compelling, tailor.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Pitch -- a short, persuasive talk to present an idea or a result
"I gave a two-minute pitch to the leadership team." (Discurso de venda)
Value proposition -- the main reason your product is useful and worth the money
"Our value proposition is faster, safer cloud services." (Proposta de valor)
Highlight -- to give special attention to something important
"I want to highlight one key result this quarter." (Destacar)
Audience -- the people who listen to a presentation or pitch
"Know your audience before you present." (Público)
Takeaway -- the main message you want people to remember
"The takeaway is simple: we have doubled our deals." (Mensagem principal)
Impact -- a strong effect or result on people or business
"Our project had a real impact on costs." (Impacto)
Persuade -- to make someone agree by giving good reasons
"A clear pitch helps you persuade the board." (Persuadir)
Outcome -- the final result of an action or process
"The outcome of the deal was a three-year contract." (Resultado)
Compelling -- very interesting and convincing
"She told a compelling story about the customer." (Convincente)
Tailor -- to adapt something for a specific person or need
"Tailor your pitch to the people in the room." (Adaptar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Pitch
Value proposition
Highlight
Audience
Takeaway
Impact
Persuade
Outcome
Compelling
Tailor

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie is preparing a two-minute pitch for the leadership review. "This quarter we have closed a lot of deals," she begins. "We have signed several new clients, and our team has delivered plenty of value." She knows her audience, so she has tailored the pitch for the directors. Her value proposition is clear: faster, safer cloud services. To persuade the board, she highlights one strong number and ends with a single takeaway: the public pipeline has grown. The outcome she wants is simple -- a yes for next year.

1. What has the team done this quarter?
A They have closed a lot of deals and signed several new clients.
B They have lost most of their clients.
C They have not started any new deals.
2. Why has Natalie tailored the pitch?
A Because she does not know the topic.
B Because she knows her audience -- the directors.
C Because the pitch is too short.
3. What is her single takeaway?
A The public pipeline has grown.
B The team needs more time.
C The prices are too high.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect and Quantifiers

GRAMMAR

Usamos o present perfect para falar de resultados e experiências até agora, e quantificadores para medir quantidade. / We use the present perfect for results and experience up to now, and quantifiers to measure amount.

FormUseExample
have/has + past participleresults and experience up to nowI have led several deals.
many / a few (countable)things you can count: clients, dealsWe have many clients.
much / a little (uncountable)things you cannot count: progressWe have made much progress.
a lot of / plenty of (both)a large amount, both typesWe have plenty of results.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use o present perfect para resultados até agora, quando o tempo exato não importa ("We have closed ten deals"). Não use com tempo terminado: diga "this year" (não "last year"). Use many com contáveis, much com incontáveis, e a lot of / plenty of com os dois.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"This quarter we a lot of deals."
"Our team plenty of value."
"We work with public agencies."
"I have the pitch for the board."
"Our is faster, safer cloud services."
"The is clear: our pipeline has grown."

Stage 2: Put the Pitch in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a strong pitch in the correct order.

?You highlight the impact with a few strong numbers.
?You tailor the pitch for your audience.
?You end with one clear takeaway to persuade them.
?You open with your value proposition in one line.
?You share the outcome you have achieved.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

This quarter we have closed a lot of deals.
Neste trimestre fechamos muitos negócios.
Our value proposition is faster, safer cloud services.
Nossa proposta de valor é um serviço de nuvem mais rápido e seguro.
We have signed several new clients.
Assinamos com vários clientes novos.
I have tailored the pitch for the audience.
Adaptei o discurso para o público.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to talk about results up to now. Which is correct?
A "We have closed many deals last year."
B "We have closed many deals this year."
C "We have closing many deals this year."
You count your clients. Which quantifier is correct?
A "We have several new clients."
B "We have much new clients."
C "We have a little new clients."
You want the board to remember one idea. What do you give them?
A one clear takeaway
B a long report
C many small details

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give a two-minute elevator pitch about a real result at Microsoft. State your value proposition, two achievements with the present perfect ("We have..."), a quantifier (several / a lot of / plenty of), and one clear takeaway. Use new words like pitch, audience, impact, outcome and tailor.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1This quarter we have closed a lot of deals.Neste trimestre fechamos muitos negócios.
2Our value proposition is faster, safer cloud services.Nossa proposta de valor é um serviço de nuvem mais rápido e seguro.
3We have signed several new clients.Assinamos com vários clientes novos.
4The takeaway is clear: our pipeline has grown.A mensagem principal é clara: nosso pipeline cresceu.
5I have tailored the pitch for the audience.Adaptei o discurso para o público.
Aula 9 -- Pre-class

Agreeing and Disagreeing -- Linking Words for Contrast

Take part in a professional debate: agree, disagree with respect, and connect your ideas with linking words (however, although, even though, on the other hand). New vocabulary: debate, standpoint, counterargument, compromise, valid, concede, common ground, reasonable, objection, consensus.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Debate -- a formal discussion where people present opposing ideas
"We had a healthy debate about the new tool." (Debate)
Standpoint -- your position or opinion on a topic or issue
"From my standpoint, we should change the tool." (Ponto de vista)
Counterargument -- a reason that goes against another person's idea
"That is a fair counterargument, but think about cost." (Contra-argumento)
Compromise -- an agreement where each side gives up something
"A small pilot is a good compromise for both teams." (Meio-termo)
Valid -- based on good reasons and worth accepting
"You make a valid point about the training time." (Válido)
Concede -- to admit that something is true or that someone is right
"I concede that the training will take time." (Admitir)
Common ground -- the ideas or interests that both sides share and agree on
"We disagree on the date, but we can find common ground." (Ponto em comum)
Reasonable -- fair, sensible and based on good judgment
"That sounds like a reasonable offer to me." (Razoável)
Objection -- a reason or statement that shows you disagree
"My only objection is the high cost." (Objeção)
Consensus -- a general agreement shared by a group of people
"After the debate, we reached a consensus." (Consenso)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Debate
Standpoint
Counterargument
Compromise
Valid
Concede
Common ground
Reasonable
Objection
Consensus

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

In the Thursday debate, Natalie takes a clear standpoint: the team should change the project tool. "The new tool saves hours," she says. "However, I know there is an objection about training time." A colleague gives a counterargument about the cost. "That is a valid point," Natalie answers, "and I concede it. Although the training takes time, the benefit is bigger." To find common ground, she offers a reasonable compromise: a small pilot first. In the end, the team reaches a consensus.

1. What is Natalie's standpoint?
A The team should change the project tool.
B The team should keep the old system forever.
C The team should stop the project.
2. How does Natalie answer the counterargument?
A She ignores it and repeats her idea.
B She concedes the valid point, then explains the bigger benefit.
C She gets angry and leaves the meeting.
3. What compromise does she offer?
A A small pilot first.
B No change at all.
C Change everything in one day.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Linking Words for Contrast

GRAMMAR

Usamos linking words para conectar duas ideias que contrastam -- concordar numa parte e discordar na outra. / We use linking words to connect two ideas that contrast -- agreeing on one part and disagreeing on another.

Linking wordUseExample
howevercontrast between two sentences (period/semicolon before)"It is a good plan. However, it is expensive."
although / even thoughconcession at the start of a clause"Although I agree, I have one objection."
on the other handintroduce the opposite side"On the other hand, the cost is high."
but / whilecontrast inside one sentence"I see your point, but I disagree."

Key point / Ponto-chave: however e on the other hand conectam duas frases (ponto ou ponto-e-vírgula antes, vírgula depois). Although / even though começam uma cláusula ("Although I agree, ..."). Nunca use although e but juntos no mesmo contraste.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The tool is powerful. , it is expensive."
" I agree with you, I have one objection."
"That is a counterargument about the cost."
"I your point about the training time."
"Let us find a reasonable ."
"After the debate, the team reached a ."

Stage 2: Put the Debate in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of disagreeing well in the correct order.

?You state your objection with a clear reason.
?You listen to the other standpoint with respect.
?You offer a reasonable compromise to reach common ground.
?You concede any valid points in their argument.
?You use a linking word like however to show the contrast.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

From my standpoint, we should change the tool.
Do meu ponto de vista, deveríamos mudar a ferramenta.
That is a valid point; however, I have one objection.
É um ponto válido; no entanto, tenho uma objeção.
I concede that, although the long-term benefit is bigger.
Admito isso, embora o benefício de longo prazo seja maior.
Even though we disagree, we can find common ground.
Mesmo discordando, podemos encontrar um ponto em comum.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You agree with one part but want to add a contrast. Which is correct?
A "Although the plan is good, but it is expensive."
B "The plan is good. However, it is expensive."
C "The plan is good, however is expensive."
You want to disagree with respect. What do you do first?
A Concede the valid points, then explain your objection.
B Say the other person is completely wrong.
C Ignore the counterargument.
The two sides want to agree. What do you look for?
A Common ground and a reasonable compromise.
B A way to win the debate.
C A longer argument.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Take a standpoint on a real decision at Microsoft. Defend it: agree where you can, concede one valid counterargument, and use linking words ("however", "although") to disagree. End with a reasonable compromise. Use new words like debate, objection, common ground and consensus.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1From my standpoint, we should change the tool.Do meu ponto de vista, deveríamos mudar a ferramenta.
2That is a valid point; however, I have one objection.É um ponto válido; no entanto, tenho uma objeção.
3I concede that, although the long-term benefit is bigger.Admito isso, embora o benefício de longo prazo seja maior.
4Even though we disagree, we can find common ground.Mesmo discordando, podemos encontrar um ponto em comum.
5Let us find a reasonable compromise and reach a consensus.Vamos encontrar um meio-termo razoável e chegar a um consenso.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

Handling Difficult Conversations -- Giving and Receiving Feedback

Give and receive feedback in a calm, professional way, and report what people said with reported speech (say / tell / ask). New vocabulary: feedback, constructive, acknowledge, defensive, tactful, address, reassure, defuse, underperform, accountability.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Feedback -- comments about someone's work, meant to help them improve
"I asked my manager for honest feedback." (Retorno)
Constructive -- helpful and meant to improve, not just to criticize
"She gave constructive feedback, not just complaints." (Construtivo)
Acknowledge -- to accept or admit that something is true
"I acknowledge that I missed one deadline." (Reconhecer)
Defensive -- reacting as if you are attacked, instead of listening
"Try not to be defensive when you receive feedback." (Defensivo)
Tactful -- careful not to upset or offend other people
"Be tactful when you point out a mistake." (Diplomático)
Address -- to deal with a problem or issue directly
"We need to address this issue calmly." (Tratar)
Reassure -- to remove someone's worry and make them feel calm
"I reassured her that the project was on track." (Tranquilizar)
Defuse -- to make a tense or difficult situation calmer
"A calm tone helps defuse a difficult conversation." (Acalmar)
Underperform -- to work below the level that was expected
"The team underperformed last quarter." (Render abaixo do esperado)
Accountability -- taking responsibility for your results and actions
"Good feedback builds accountability and trust." (Responsabilização)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Feedback
Constructive
Acknowledge
Defensive
Tactful
Address
Reassure
Defuse
Underperform
Accountability

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After her quarterly review, Natalie writes a short note. Her manager said that her work was strong and the client was very happy. He also told her that she sometimes sounded defensive. He asked her to pause and listen first. Natalie did not argue; she acknowledged the valid point and gave a constructive answer. She said that she would take accountability. It was a difficult conversation, but a tactful and calm one.

1. What did the manager say about Natalie's work?
A It was strong, and the client was very happy.
B It was weak and the client was angry.
C It was late and full of mistakes.
2. What did he ask her to do?
A To answer faster and defend herself.
B To pause and listen first.
C To change the whole report.
3. How did Natalie react to the feedback?
A She acknowledged the valid point and answered constructively.
B She got defensive and argued.
C She left the meeting.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Usamos reported speech para dizer o que outra pessoa falou. O verbo costuma voltar um passo no tempo. / We use reported speech to say what another person said. The verb usually moves one step back in time.

StructureUseExample
say (that) ...report a statement (no person after say)"He said that the report was clear."
tell + somebody (that) ...report a statement to a person"She told me that I needed more data."
tell + sb + to + verbreport an instruction"My manager told me to add detail."
ask + sb + to + verbreport a request"She asked me to send it again."

Key point / Ponto-chave: o verbo volta um passo (is → was, need → needed, will → would, can → could). Use tell + pessoa ("told me"), mas say sem pessoa ("said that"). Nunca diga "said me" nem "told to me".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"He that the client was happy."
"She me that I needed more data."
"My manager me to send it again."
"He said that the deadline too short."
"I want honest, constructive ."
"Good feedback builds trust and ."

Stage 2: Put the Feedback in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of giving good feedback in the correct order.

?You stay calm and tactful, so the other person does not feel attacked.
?You start with something positive and specific that worked well.
?You agree on a clear next step and reassure the person about the goal.
?You address one issue at a time, not everything at once.
?You listen and acknowledge any valid points the person makes.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Thank you for the honest, constructive feedback.
Obrigada pelo feedback honesto e construtivo.
He told me that my report was clear and specific.
Ele me disse que meu relatório estava claro e específico.
I acknowledge that, and I will address it this week.
Reconheço isso e vou tratar disso esta semana.
She asked me to pause and listen before I answer.
Ela me pediu para pausar e ouvir antes de responder.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You report what your boss said. Which is correct?
A "He said me that I improved."
B "He told me that I improved."
C "He told to me that I improved."
You receive critical feedback. What is the best reaction?
A Get defensive and argue immediately.
B Acknowledge the valid point and ask questions.
C Ignore it and change the subject.
You give feedback to a teammate. What is the best approach?
A List every mistake at once.
B Start positive, then address one issue tactfully.
C Attack the person, not the work.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Report two things your manager once told you, using reported speech ("He said that...", "She told me to..."). Then give one piece of constructive feedback to a teammate: start with something positive and address one issue. Use new words like feedback, acknowledge, reassure and accountability.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1Thank you for the honest, constructive feedback.Obrigada pelo feedback honesto e construtivo.
2He told me that my report was clear and specific.Ele me disse que meu relatório estava claro e específico.
3I acknowledge that, and I will address it this week.Reconheço isso e vou tratar disso esta semana.
4Let me reassure you: I am here to help, not to criticize.Deixe-me tranquilizá-lo: estou aqui para ajudar, não para criticar.
5She asked me to pause and listen before I answer.Ela me pediu para pausar e ouvir antes de responder.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Building Trust -- Networking and Professional Relationships

Start genuine professional relationships and confirm information with question tags (don't you?, haven't we?, aren't you?). New vocabulary: rapport, mutual, reliable, referral, reach out, genuine, acquaintance, credibility, nurture, approachable.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Rapport -- a friendly relationship in which people understand each other
"We built a good rapport at the conference." (Sintonia)
Mutual -- shared or felt by two or more people
"We have a mutual interest in cloud solutions." (Mútuo)
Reliable -- able to be trusted to do what is expected
"She is a reliable partner; she always delivers." (Confiável)
Referral -- when you recommend someone to one of your contacts
"I got the meeting through a referral." (Indicação)
Reach out -- to make contact with someone, often to start a relationship
"I will reach out to him after the event." (Entrar em contato)
Genuine -- sincere and honest, not pretended or fake
"She showed genuine interest in my work." (Genuíno)
Acquaintance -- a person you know a little, but who is not a close friend
"He is a business acquaintance, not a friend yet." (Conhecido)
Credibility -- the quality of being trusted and believed in
"Good results build your credibility." (Credibilidade)
Nurture -- to care for a relationship and help it grow over time
"You have to nurture professional relationships." (Cultivar)
Approachable -- friendly and easy to talk to
"A good leader is approachable." (Acessível)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Rapport
Mutual
Reliable
Referral
Reach out
Genuine
Acquaintance
Credibility
Nurture
Approachable

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At a tech conference, Natalie meets Tom. They build a good rapport very quickly. "You work with cloud solutions, don't you?" Tom asks. They share a mutual interest, so they decide to reach out after the event. Natalie shows genuine interest and offers a referral to a reliable colleague. Tom is approachable, and his strong results give him credibility. "We've met at the right time, haven't we?" he says. They agree to nurture the connection, because a new acquaintance can become a trusted partner over time.

1. How do Natalie and Tom feel about each other?
A They build a good rapport very quickly.
B They do not trust each other at all.
C They argue about cloud solutions.
2. What do they decide to do after the event?
A To forget about each other.
B To reach out and stay in touch.
C To compete for the same client.
3. What does Natalie offer Tom?
A A referral to a reliable colleague.
B A complaint about her manager.
C Nothing at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Question Tags

GRAMMAR

Usamos question tags (mini-perguntas no fim da frase) para confirmar algo e convidar o outro a responder. Frase positiva leva tag negativo, e vice-versa. / We use question tags to confirm something and invite the other person to respond. A positive sentence takes a negative tag, and a negative sentence takes a positive tag.

StatementTagExample
positive (be)negative: aren't / isn't"You're new here, aren't you?"
positive (present simple)negative: don't / doesn't"You work in tech, don't you?"
positive (present perfect)negative: haven't / hasn't"We've met before, haven't we?"
negative statementpositive tag"You don't know Sarah, do you?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: o tag usa o mesmo auxiliar da frase (be → be, have → have, present simple → do/does, past → did) e o mesmo pronome do sujeito. Frase positiva → tag negativo; frase negativa → tag positivo. Nunca use "isn't it?" com um sujeito como you ("aren't you?").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct tag or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"You work in tech, ?"
"We've met before, ?"
"You're not leaving yet, ?"
"She knows Tom, ?"
"It was great to build with you."
"A strong network is built on trust and ."

Stage 2: Build a Relationship in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of building a professional relationship in the correct order.

?You follow up soon, while the connection is still fresh.
?You start a genuine conversation and find a mutual interest.
?You nurture the relationship over time, so it stays reliable.
?You exchange contacts and agree to reach out.
?You offer real value, like a referral or useful information.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

It's a great event for networking, isn't it?
É um ótimo evento para networking, não é?
I think we have a mutual interest in this area.
Acho que temos um interesse mútuo nesta área.
I could give you a referral; she is very reliable.
Eu poderia te dar uma indicação; ela é muito confiável.
Let's stay in touch and nurture this connection.
Vamos manter contato e cultivar essa conexão.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to confirm something and keep the conversation going. Which is best?
A "You work in tech, isn't it?"
B "You work in tech, don't you?"
C "You work in tech, do you not?"
You meet someone new at an event. What is the best first move?
A Only collect as many business cards as you can.
B Show genuine interest and find a mutual topic.
C Talk only about yourself the whole time.
After a good first meeting, what builds a lasting relationship?
A Wait for them to contact you first, every time.
B Reach out, follow up, and nurture it over time.
C Never contact the person again.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real networking situation at Microsoft or an event. Explain how you would build rapport with a new acquaintance. Use question tags to confirm and connect ("You work in...,don't you?"), and new words like mutual, genuine, referral, reliable and nurture.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1It's a great event for networking, isn't it?É um ótimo evento para networking, não é?
2I think we have a mutual interest in this area.Acho que temos um interesse mútuo nesta área.
3Let's stay in touch; I'll reach out next week.Vamos manter contato; eu entro em contato semana que vem.
4I could give you a referral; she is very reliable.Eu poderia te dar uma indicação; ela é muito confiável.
5It was a genuine pleasure to meet you. Let's nurture this connection.Foi um prazer genuíno te conhecer. Vamos cultivar essa conexão.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Review -- Integration Check

Bring the first half together: recall key words and review the structures you already own (present perfect, passive voice, first conditional, comparatives, question tags). Review vocabulary: agenda, draft, tender, pitch, outcome, compromise, common ground, feedback, acknowledge, rapport.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Recall

Vocabulary

These are key words from your first eleven lessons. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agenda -- a list of the topics to discuss in a meeting
"Let's review the agenda before we start." (Pauta)
Draft -- an early version of a document you will revise
"I sent the first draft of the report this morning." (Rascunho)
Tender -- a formal invitation for companies to submit bids
"We submitted our bid for the public tender." (Licitação)
Pitch -- a short, persuasive presentation of an idea or product
"My pitch convinced the leadership team." (Apresentação de venda)
Outcome -- the final result or effect of an action or process
"The outcome of the meeting was very positive." (Resultado)
Compromise -- an agreement where each side gives up something
"We reached a fair compromise on the price." (Acordo mútuo)
Common ground -- a topic or opinion that two sides share and agree on
"We found common ground on the deadline." (Ponto em comum)
Feedback -- helpful information about how well someone is doing
"Thank you for the constructive feedback." (Retorno / devolutiva)
Acknowledge -- to recognize or admit that something is true or valid
"I acknowledge your point; it is a fair one." (Reconhecer)
Rapport -- a friendly relationship in which people understand each other
"We built a strong rapport with the client." (Sintonia)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Agenda
Draft
Tender
Pitch
Outcome
Compromise
Common ground
Feedback
Acknowledge
Rapport

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the midpoint of the cloud project, Natalie prepares an update. The team has already delivered the new platform, and the client is happy. The proposal was approved by procurement, so they can move forward. This quarter is stronger than the last one. "If we keep this pace, we will hit our target early," she says. In the meeting, she sets the agenda, presents the outcome, and asks for feedback. She builds rapport with everyone and finds common ground quickly. "You lead this project, don't you?" the director asks. "I do," Natalie answers, and she acknowledges every comment with confidence.

1. What has the team already done?
A Delivered the new platform.
B Cancelled the project.
C Lost the client.
2. Why can they move forward?
A Because the agenda was lost.
B Because the proposal was approved by procurement.
C Because the client refused the offer.
3. How does Natalie respond to comments?
A She acknowledges every comment with confidence.
B She ignores all the feedback.
C She leaves the meeting early.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Structure Map

GRAMMAR

Esta aula nao traz gramatica nova: ela revisa as estruturas que voce ja aprendeu. Voce escolhe cada estrutura por um motivo. / This lesson has no new grammar: it reviews the structures you already learned. You choose each structure for a reason.

StructureUse it forExample
Present perfectexperience with a result now"I have presented the data."
Passive voicethe action matters, not who did it"The proposal was approved."
First conditionala real future possibility"If we keep this pace, we will win."
Comparativescomparing two things"This quarter is stronger than the last."
Question tagsconfirm and connect"You work in tech, don't you?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: a voz passiva e o present perfect usam o past participle (approved, written, presented), nunca o verbo base. O comparativo usa -er than (stronger than) ou more + adjetivo (more confident), nunca os dois juntos. O question tag combina com o auxiliar e o pronome da frase. / The passive and the present perfect use the past participle; comparatives use -er than or more, never both.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct structure or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We the new platform."
"The contract last week."
"If the client agrees, we in May."
"This quarter is the last one."
"You lead the project, ?"
"Thank you for the constructive ."

Stage 2: Run a Review Meeting in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a midpoint review meeting in the correct order.

?You give honest feedback and listen to the other person.
?You set a clear objective for the meeting.
?You reach out to follow up and nurture the relationship over time.
?You present the data, comparing this quarter with the last one.
?You agree on the next steps and confirm them with a question tag.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

We have already delivered the project, and the client is happy.
Nós já entregamos o projeto, e o cliente está satisfeito.
The proposal was approved, so we can move forward.
A proposta foi aprovada, então podemos seguir em frente.
If we keep this pace, we will hit our target early.
Se mantivermos esse ritmo, vamos atingir a meta mais cedo.
This quarter is stronger than the last one, isn't it?
Este trimestre está mais forte que o anterior, não está?
