Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to report a result that still matters now. Which is best?
A "We delivered the platform yesterday at 3 p.m."
B "We have already delivered the platform."
C "We delivering the platform now."
You want to focus on the action, not on who did it. Which is best?
A "The proposal was approved by procurement."
B "Procurement, they did approve the proposal, yes."
C "The proposal was approve by procurement."
You want to confirm a shared plan and invite a reply. Which is best?
A "We are on the same page, isn't it?"
B "We are on the same page, aren't we?"
C "We are on the same page, don't we?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute midpoint self-assessment. Describe what you have learned in the first half (use the present perfect: I have learned to...), compare yourself with how you started (use comparatives: I am more confident than before), and set one goal for the second half (use the first conditional: if I keep this pace, I will...).

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1We have already delivered the project, and the client is happy.Nós já entregamos o projeto, e o cliente está satisfeito.
2The proposal was approved, so we can move forward.A proposta foi aprovada, então podemos seguir em frente.
3If we keep this pace, we will hit our target early.Se mantivermos esse ritmo, vamos atingir a meta mais cedo.
4This quarter is stronger than the last one, isn't it?Este trimestre está mais forte que o anterior, não está?
5Thank you for the feedback; I acknowledge it and I agree.Obrigada pelo retorno; eu reconheço e concordo.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

Negotiation Basics -- Finding Common Ground

Learn the language of negotiation and the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base verb) to talk about imaginary deals and find a win-win. New vocabulary: leverage, trade-off, bottom line, terms, concession, counteroffer, deadlock, walk away, settle, win-win.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary

Vocabulary

These are the key words of negotiation. Listen to each one and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Leverage -- an advantage that gives you power in a negotiation
"Our early delivery gives us real leverage." (Vantagem / poder de barganha)
Trade-off -- a balance where you accept less of one thing for more of another
"A longer contract for a lower price is a fair trade-off." (Troca / contrapartida)
Bottom line -- the lowest price or limit you are willing to accept
"Twelve percent is our bottom line; we cannot go lower." (Limite mínimo)
Terms -- the conditions that both sides agree to in a deal
"We accepted the terms and signed the deal." (Condições / termos)
Concession -- something you give up in order to move closer to an agreement
"I made a small concession on the price." (Concessão)
Counteroffer -- a new offer you make in response to the other side's offer
"We rejected their first price and made a counteroffer." (Contraproposta)
Deadlock -- a situation where neither side will move and the talks are stuck
"We almost reached a deadlock over the timeline." (Impasse)
Walk away -- to leave a negotiation without reaching an agreement
"If the terms are bad, we are ready to walk away." (Desistir / sair sem acordo)
Settle -- to finally agree on something after discussion
"We settled on a fair number for both sides." (Fechar acordo)
Win-win -- a result in which both sides get something they value
"A longer contract was a true win-win for both companies." (Ganha-ganha)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Leverage
Trade-off
Bottom line
Terms
Concession
Counteroffer
Deadlock
Walk away
Settle
Win-win

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie is preparing for a tough negotiation with a government client. Their main concern is the price, so the bottom line will be hard. But Natalie has one big advantage: her company is the only vendor that can deliver on time, and that is real leverage. She plans a smart trade-off. "If they asked for a lower price, I would offer a longer contract," she thinks. She knows the rule: never make a concession without getting something back. If the talks reached a deadlock, she would suggest a short break instead of choosing to walk away. Her goal is not to win everything; it is to find common ground and settle on a deal that feels like a win-win for both sides.

1. What is the client's main concern?
A The price (the bottom line).
B The color of the logo.
C The name of the project.
2. What would Natalie do if they asked for a lower price?
A She would walk away immediately.
B She would offer a longer contract in return.
C She would accept any price.
3. What is Natalie's real goal?
A To find common ground and settle on a win-win.
B To win everything and give nothing.
C To end the meeting as fast as possible.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

O segundo condicional fala de situações imaginárias ou hipotéticas: if + passado simples + would + verbo base. É a linguagem da negociação porque soa mais educada e estratégica. / The second conditional talks about imaginary or hypothetical situations: if + past simple, would + base verb.

FormExample (EN)Tradução (PT)
Affirmative"If they asked, we would offer more time."Se eles pedissem, nós ofereceríamos mais tempo.
Negative"If we lowered it, we would not make a profit."Se baixássemos, não teríamos lucro.
Advice (were)"If I were you, I would make a counteroffer."Se eu fosse você, eu faria uma contraproposta.
Question"What would you do if the talks reached a deadlock?"O que você faria se as conversas chegassem a um impasse?

Key point / Ponto-chave: nunca use would depois de if — a parte do "if" leva o passado simples (asked, lowered, found), e só a parte do resultado usa would + verbo base. Depois de "if", use were para todos os sujeitos: "If I were you." Compare com o primeiro condicional (real): "If they ask, we will offer" (possível) vs "If they asked, we would offer" (imaginário). / Never use would after if; use the past simple in the if-clause and were for all subjects.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If they asked for a discount, we a longer contract."
"If we the price, we would lose our margin."
"If I you, I would make a counteroffer."
"Our early delivery gives us real ."
"A longer contract for a lower price is a fair ."
"We found common ground, so we can on a fair number."

Stage 2: Run a Negotiation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a smart negotiation in the correct order.

?You make a small concession only if you get something back in return.
?You prepare and decide your bottom line before the meeting.
?You confirm the terms and settle on a deal that is a real win-win.
?You open the conversation and listen for the other side's priorities.
?You propose a trade-off instead of just lowering your price.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

If you signed a longer contract, I would lower the price.
Se você assinasse um contrato mais longo, eu baixaria o preço.
That is our bottom line; we cannot go lower.
Esse é o nosso limite mínimo; não podemos baixar mais.
If I were you, I would make a counteroffer.
Se eu fosse você, eu faria uma contraproposta.
Let's find common ground so this becomes a win-win.
Vamos encontrar um ponto em comum para que isso seja um ganha-ganha.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to offer a trade-off about an imaginary deal. Which is best?
A "If they would ask for less, we would offer more time."
B "If they asked for less, we would offer more time."
C "If they ask for less, we would offered more time."
You want to give polite advice to a colleague. Which is best?
A "If I were you, I would make a counteroffer."
B "If I was you, I will make a counteroffer."
C "If I am you, I make a counteroffer."
You want to close a deal that is good for both sides. Which is best?
A "We will walk away and they will lose everything."
B "We found common ground, so we can settle on a fair win-win."
C "We reached a deadlock and ended the talks."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Record a two-minute negotiation summary about a real or imaginary deal. State your bottom line, offer a trade-off using the second conditional (if they asked for X, I would offer Y), and explain how you would find common ground and settle on a win-win.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1If you signed a longer contract, I would lower the price.Se você assinasse um contrato mais longo, eu baixaria o preço.
2That is our bottom line; we cannot go lower.Esse é o nosso limite mínimo; não podemos baixar mais.
3If I were you, I would make a counteroffer.Se eu fosse você, eu faria uma contraproposta.
4Let's find common ground so this becomes a win-win.Vamos encontrar um ponto em comum para que isso seja um ganha-ganha.
5I think we can settle on these terms.Acho que podemos fechar acordo com essas condições.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

Closing the Deal -- Confirming Agreements

Close a deal, confirm what was agreed, and report what people said with reported speech (said / told + backshift: is→was, will→would). New vocabulary: finalize, sign off, commitment, contract, paperwork, on the same page, going forward, deal-breaker, formalize, kick off.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Finalize -- to complete the last details and make something final
"Let's finalize the contract this week." (Finalizar)
Sign off -- to give formal approval to a document or decision
"Procurement will sign off on the contract by Friday." (Aprovar formalmente)
Commitment -- a serious promise or obligation to do something
"We made a clear commitment to deliver on time." (Compromisso)
Contract -- a written legal agreement between two or more parties
"Both sides signed the contract yesterday." (Contrato)
Paperwork -- the documents and forms you must complete for something official
"The paperwork was almost ready." (Papelada / documentação)
On the same page -- in agreement, understanding a situation the same way
"Let's repeat the terms so we are on the same page." (Em sintonia / de acordo)
Going forward -- from now on; in the future, after this point
"Going forward, we will send weekly updates." (Daqui em diante)
Deal-breaker -- a problem so serious that it can stop an agreement
"A late delivery would be a deal-breaker." (Impedimento decisivo)
Formalize -- to make an agreement official, usually in writing
"We will formalize the deal with a signed contract." (Formalizar)
Kick off -- to start a project, process, or meeting
"We will kick off the project in early March." (Dar início)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Finalize
Sign off
Commitment
Contract
Paperwork
On the same page
Going forward
Deal-breaker
Formalize
Kick off

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After a long negotiation, Natalie finally closes the deal with Tom. He calls to confirm everything. He said that the terms were perfect, and he told me that procurement would sign off on the contract by Friday. The paperwork is almost ready, so they are on the same page. There are no deal-breakers left. "We have a clear commitment from both sides," Natalie thinks. They agree to finalize the last details on a short call and to formalize the agreement with a signed contract. He also said that they could kick off the project in early March. Going forward, Natalie will send a weekly update, so the client always knows the status.

1. What did Tom say about the terms?
A He said that the terms were perfect.
B He said the terms were a problem.
C He cancelled the deal.
2. What will procurement do by Friday?
A Reject the proposal.
B Sign off on the contract.
C Start a new negotiation.
3. When can they kick off the project?
A In early March.
B Never.
C Last year.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Usamos reported speech para contar o que alguém disse. Usamos said (sem pessoa) ou told + pessoa, e o verbo recua um tempo. / We use reported speech to tell what someone said. We use said (no person) or told + person, and the verb shifts back one tense.

DirectShiftReported
"are perfect"are → wereHe said the terms were perfect.
"will sign off"will → wouldHe told me they would sign off.
"can kick off"can → couldShe said we could kick off in March.
"have signed"have → hadShe said she had signed it.

Key point / Ponto-chave: use told com a pessoa (told me) e said sem a pessoa (said that). O verbo recua um tempo: is/are → was/were, will → would, can → could, have done → had done. Nunca diga "he said me".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct reported form or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The price is final." → He said the price final.
"I will sign off." → She told me she sign off.
"We can kick off in March." → He said we kick off in March.
"He ___ me that the deal was ready." (told or said?)
"Once procurement signs off, we will the paperwork."
"Let's repeat the terms, so we are on the same ."

Stage 2: Close the Deal in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of closing a deal in the correct order.

?You put the agreement in writing and finalize the contract.
?Both sides reach an agreement and shake hands on the main terms.
?You confirm the start date and kick off the project.
?You repeat the key points out loud, so everyone is on the same page.
?The right people review and sign off on the paperwork.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me confirm the terms, so we are on the same page.
Deixe-me confirmar os termos, para ficarmos em sintonia.
He said the terms were perfect, and he told me procurement would sign off.
Ele disse que os termos estavam perfeitos, e me disse que o setor de compras aprovaria.
Once procurement signs off, we will finalize the paperwork.
Assim que compras aprovar, vamos finalizar a documentação.
Going forward, we will kick off the project in early March.
Daqui em diante, vamos dar início ao projeto no começo de março.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to report what the client said about the terms. Which is correct?
A "He said me the terms are perfect."
B "He told me the terms were perfect."
C "He told the terms are perfect."
You just agreed verbally. What makes the deal real and official?
A Just a friendly handshake, nothing more.
B Finalizing the paperwork and signing the contract.
C Forgetting about the details.
You want to make sure both sides understand the deal the same way. What do you say?
A "I won't repeat anything; it's clear enough."
B "Let me confirm the terms, so we are on the same page."
C "Let's change all the terms now."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real deal or project you closed at Microsoft. How did you confirm the agreement and finalize the paperwork? Report what people said using reported speech ("He said that...", "She told me that..."), and use new words like contract, sign off, commitment, finalize and kick off.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Let me confirm the terms, so we are on the same page.Deixe-me confirmar os termos, para ficarmos em sintonia.
2He said the terms were perfect, and he told me procurement would sign off.Ele disse que os termos estavam perfeitos, e me disse que compras aprovaria.
3Once procurement signs off, we'll finalize the paperwork.Assim que compras aprovar, vamos finalizar a documentação.
4We have a clear commitment from both sides.Temos um compromisso claro de ambos os lados.
5Going forward, we'll kick off the project in early March.Daqui em diante, vamos dar início ao projeto no começo de março.
Aula 15 -- Pre-class

Project Kick-off -- Giving Updates

Run a project kick-off, give updates, and describe what will be happening with the future continuous (will be + verb-ing: "Next week I will be onboarding the team"). New vocabulary: onboard, timeline, workstream, owner, assign, status update, progress, on track, bottleneck, phase.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Onboard -- to bring a new person or client into a project and get them ready
"We will onboard the new users next week." (Integrar)
Timeline -- the planned order and dates of the steps in a project
"The timeline is tight, but realistic." (Cronograma)
Workstream -- one separate part of a project, with its own tasks and goal
"Each workstream has a clear owner." (Frente de trabalho)
Owner -- the person responsible for a task or part of the project
"Every deliverable needs a clear owner." (Responsável)
Assign -- to give a task or responsibility to a specific person
"I will assign the data workstream to Maria." (Atribuir)
Status update -- a short report on how the work is going right now
"I will lead the weekly status update." (Atualização de status)
Progress -- movement forward toward a goal; how much is done
"We will review progress at the end of each phase." (Progresso)
On track -- going as planned, according to the schedule
"The first sprint is on track for Friday." (No prazo / dentro do plano)
Bottleneck -- a point in the process that slows down all the rest of the work
"The data migration could become a bottleneck." (Gargalo)
Phase -- one distinct stage of a project, with a start and an end
"We will wrap up the first phase in April." (Fase / etapa)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Onboard
Timeline
Workstream
Owner
Assign
Status update
Progress
On track
Bottleneck
Phase

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The deal is signed, and the project starts now. At the kick-off, Natalie walks the team through the timeline and the workstreams. She gives each one a clear owner. "Next week, the team will be onboarding the new users," she explains, "and I will be leading the weekly status update, so we always know if we are on track." Tom is worried that the data migration could become a bottleneck, but Natalie says they will be monitoring it from day one. By Friday, the team will be running the first sprint, and they will be tracking progress against every milestone. This time next month, they will be wrapping up the first phase and reviewing the results.

1. What will the team be doing next week?
A They will be onboarding the new users.
B They will be closing the office.
C They will be on holiday.
2. Why is Natalie worried about the data migration?
A It is already finished.
B It could become a bottleneck.
C It has no owner.
3. What will they be doing this time next month?
A Wrapping up the first phase.
B Starting from zero again.
C Cancelling the project.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Future Continuous

GRAMMAR

Usamos o future continuous para descrever uma ação EM ANDAMENTO num momento futuro, ou um plano já organizado. A forma é will be + verbo-ing, igual para todos os sujeitos. / We use the future continuous for an action in progress at a future moment, or a planned part of the work. The form is will be + verb-ing, the same for every subject.

SubjectFormExample
Iwill be + -ingNext week I will be onboarding the team.
Shewill be + -ingIn March she will be leading the calls.
Wewill be + -ingWe will be wrapping up phase one.
Theywill be + -ingThey will be running the first sprint.

Key point / Ponto-chave: sempre mantenha o be: will be + verbo-ing. Nunca diga "will onboarding" nem "will being onboarding". Compare: "I will send the report" (ação única, completa) vs "I will be sending weekly reports" (ação em andamento ao longo do tempo).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the future continuous or the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

Next week, I the new users.
In March, she the status calls.
By Friday, the team the first sprint.
I will an owner to each workstream.
We will watch the migration, because it could become a .
The first sprint is for Friday.

Stage 2: Kick Off in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a project kick-off in the correct order.

?You agree on the timeline and the key milestones.
?You confirm the scope and the main deliverables with the client.
?You start tracking progress and watching for any bottleneck.
?You assign an owner to each workstream, so everyone knows their part.
?You hold the kick-off call and explain what each person will be working on.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me walk you through the timeline and the workstreams.
Deixe-me apresentar o cronograma e as frentes de trabalho.
Next week, the team will be onboarding the new users.
Na próxima semana, a equipe estará integrando os novos usuários.
I will be leading the weekly status update to keep us on track.
Eu estarei conduzindo a atualização semanal de status para mantermos tudo no prazo.
This time next month, we will be wrapping up the first phase.
Neste mesmo período do próximo mês, estaremos encerrando a primeira fase.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to say what you will be doing next week, in progress. Which is correct?
A "Next week I will onboarding the team."
B "Next week I will be onboarding the team."
C "Next week I will being onboard the team."
You want everyone to know who is responsible for each part. What do you do?
A Leave every workstream without an owner.
B Assign a clear owner to each workstream.
C Ask everyone to do everything.
The data migration is slowing everything down. What is it?
A A milestone.
B A bottleneck.
C A status update.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe a real project you are starting at Microsoft. How would you kick it off? Use the future continuous to say what you and your team will be doing over the next month ("Next week I will be...", "In March the team will be..."), and use new words like timeline, owner, workstream, status update and bottleneck.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1Let me walk you through the timeline and the workstreams.Deixe-me apresentar o cronograma e as frentes de trabalho.
2Next week, the team will be onboarding the new users.Na próxima semana, a equipe estará integrando os novos usuários.
3I will be leading the weekly status update to keep us on track.Eu estarei conduzindo a atualização semanal de status para mantermos tudo no prazo.
4We will be monitoring the data migration, in case it becomes a bottleneck.Estaremos monitorando a migração de dados, caso ela vire um gargalo.
5This time next month, we will be wrapping up the first phase.Neste mesmo período do próximo mês, estaremos encerrando a primeira fase.
Aula 16 -- Pre-class

Project Updates -- Reporting Progress

Report progress to leadership and describe ongoing work with the present perfect continuous (have/has been + -ing, for / since). New vocabulary: metrics, to date, ahead of schedule, behind schedule, momentum, forecast, dependency, blocker, escalate, mitigate.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Metrics -- numbers used to measure and track progress or results
"The metrics show we are ahead of schedule." (Métricas)
To date -- from the beginning of the project until now
"To date, we have completed two phases." (Até agora)
Ahead of schedule -- earlier or faster than the planned time
"Good news: we are ahead of schedule this month." (Adiantado)
Behind schedule -- later or slower than the planned time
"One workstream is slightly behind schedule." (Atrasado)
Momentum -- the energy and speed that keep progress moving forward
"The team is building real momentum." (Impulso)
Forecast -- a prediction about the future based on current data
"Our forecast shows we will finish in May." (Previsão)
Dependency -- a task that must be finished before another can start
"The vendor sign-off is a dependency for the next phase." (Dependência)
Blocker -- a problem that stops the work from moving forward
"There is one blocker: we are waiting for approval." (Impedimento)
Escalate -- to raise an issue to a more senior person for help
"I will escalate the delay to the project sponsor." (Escalar)
Mitigate -- to reduce the size or impact of a risk or problem
"We have a backup plan to mitigate the risk." (Mitigar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Metrics
To date
Ahead of schedule
Behind schedule
Momentum
Forecast
Dependency
Blocker
Escalate
Mitigate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Before the leadership review, Natalie prepares her update. "We have been working on phase two for three weeks," she writes. The metrics are strong, and to date the project is ahead of schedule. The team has been testing the new environment since Monday, so they have real momentum. There is one blocker, though: the vendor sign-off is a dependency for the next phase. Natalie decides to escalate it early and to mitigate the risk with a backup plan. Her forecast still shows the project will finish ahead of plan.

1. How long has the team been working on phase two?
A For three weeks, and the work is still going on.
B They have not started phase two yet.
C They finished phase two last year.
2. What is the one blocker?
A The metrics are weak.
B The vendor sign-off, which is a dependency.
C The team has lost momentum.
3. How does Natalie plan to handle the blocker?
A Escalate it early and mitigate the risk with a backup plan.
B Hide it from leadership.
C Stop the project completely.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect Continuous

GRAMMAR

Usamos o present perfect continuous (have/has been + verbo-ing) para ações que começaram no passado e continuam agora, enfatizando a DURAÇÃO. Use for + período e since + ponto de início. / We use the present perfect continuous for actions that started in the past and are still happening, stressing duration. Use for + a period and since + a start point.

FormUseExample
have/has + been + -ingongoing, still happening"We have been working on it."
+ for + periodhow long (duration)"...for three weeks."
+ since + start pointwhen it started"...since Monday."
present perfect simplefinished result"We have completed the audit."

Key point / Ponto-chave: use have/has been + -ing para o PROCESSO em andamento (have been testing) e o present perfect simples para o RESULTADO concluído (have completed). for = período (for three weeks); since = ponto de início (since Monday). Terceira pessoa: has been (she has been monitoring), nunca "have been".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct verb form or word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We on the migration for three weeks."
"The team has been testing the system Monday."
"We have been running phase two three weeks."
"We the data security audit."
"To date, the show we are ahead of schedule."
"There is one : a vendor dependency."

Stage 2: Build a Progress Update in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a clear progress update in the correct order.

?You explain what the team has been doing and the momentum so far.
?You state the headline, so leadership knows the status in one sentence.
?You confirm the next steps and whether you are still ahead of schedule.
?You report the results you have completed, with clear metrics.
?You flag any blocker or dependency honestly, before it grows.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

To date, the metrics show we are ahead of schedule.
Até agora, as métricas mostram que estamos adiantados.
We have been working on phase two for three weeks.
Estamos trabalhando na fase dois há três semanas.
There is one blocker: a dependency I will escalate early.
Há um impedimento: uma dependência que vou escalar logo.
We have a backup plan to mitigate the risk.
Temos um plano reserva para mitigar o risco.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to describe ongoing work and how long it has lasted. Which is best?
A "We work on it since three weeks."
B "We have been working on it for three weeks."
C "We are working on it since Monday for three weeks."
Leadership asks for a status. What makes a good update?
A Only report the good news and hide every problem.
B Give the headline, the metrics, and flag one blocker honestly.
C Give every small detail with no headline.
A dependency may delay the next phase. What is the best move?
A Wait and hope the problem disappears.
B Escalate it early and mitigate the risk with a backup plan.
C Tell the team to ignore it.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give a one-minute progress update on a real project at Microsoft. Open with the headline, report results with metrics, explain what the team has been doing (use the present perfect continuous), and flag one blocker. Use new words like ahead of schedule, momentum, dependency, escalate and mitigate.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1To date, the metrics show we are ahead of schedule.Até agora, as métricas mostram que estamos adiantados.
2We have been working on phase two for three weeks.Estamos trabalhando na fase dois há três semanas.
3We have completed the audit, and we are still building momentum.Concluímos a auditoria e ainda estamos ganhando impulso.
4There is one blocker: a dependency I will escalate early.Há um impedimento: uma dependência que vou escalar logo.
5Our forecast shows we will finish ahead of plan; we have a plan to mitigate the risk.Nossa previsão mostra que terminaremos adiantados; temos um plano para mitigar o risco.
Aula 17 -- Pre-class

Leading the Status Meeting -- Moderating & Handling Questions

Lead a status meeting and ask polite, indirect questions (Could you tell me how...? I would like to know whether...?). New vocabulary: moderate, steer, summary, agenda item, raise a point, off-topic, defer, double-check, follow-up, close out.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Moderate -- to lead and guide a meeting, keeping it on track
"Natalie will moderate the status meeting this week." (Conduzir / mediar)
Steer -- to guide a discussion in the right direction
"A good moderator steers the conversation gently." (Direcionar / guiar)
Summary -- a short version of the main points so far
"Let me start with a quick summary of last week." (Resumo)
Agenda item -- one single topic on the list of things to discuss
"The next agenda item is the testing timeline." (Item da pauta)
Raise a point -- to bring up a topic or question for the group to discuss
"Thanks for raising that point." (Levantar uma questão)
Off-topic -- not connected to the topic the group is discussing now
"That is a bit off-topic, so let's park it." (Fora do assunto)
Defer -- to decide to deal with something later, not right now
"Let's defer that question to the end of the call." (Adiar / postergar)
Double-check -- to check a detail again to be sure it is correct
"Let me double-check that number and get back to you." (Conferir / verificar)
Follow-up -- a task that someone agrees to do after the meeting
"I'll log that as a follow-up with an owner." (Tarefa / ação a fazer)
Close out -- to finish and end a meeting, summarizing the next steps
"Let's close out: here are the next steps." (Encerrar / finalizar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Moderate
Steer
Summary
Agenda item
Raise a point
Off-topic
Defer
Double-check
Follow-up
Close out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie is going to moderate the status meeting. She plans to open with a quick summary, then steer each agenda item. To sound polite, she will use indirect questions: "Could you tell me how the testing is going?" and "I would like to know whether the budget is approved." When a colleague raises a point that is off-topic, she will defer it and log it as a follow-up. If she is not sure of a number, she will not guess; she will double-check it after the call. Finally, she will close out with the next steps and the owners.

1. What is the polite (indirect) way to ask about the testing?
A "Could you tell me how the testing is going?"
B "Could you tell me how is the testing going?"
C "How does the testing is going?"
2. What does Natalie do with an off-topic point?
A She stops the whole meeting to discuss it.
B She defers it and logs it as a follow-up.
C She ignores the person completely.
3. What does Natalie do if she is not sure of a number?
A She does not guess; she will double-check it after the call.
B She invents a number to look confident.
C She ends the meeting early.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Indirect Questions

GRAMMAR

Usamos perguntas indiretas para soar mais educados e profissionais. Abrimos com Could you tell me.../Do you know.../I would like to know..., e depois usamos a ORDEM NORMAL (sujeito + verbo), sem do/does/did. Para perguntas de sim/não, usamos if ou whether. / We use indirect questions to sound more polite. Open with Could you tell me.../Do you know.../I would like to know..., then use NORMAL word order (subject + verb), with no do/does/did. For yes/no questions, use if or whether.

OpenerThenExample
Could you tell me + question wordsubject + verb (normal)"...how the testing is going."
Do you know + question wordno do/does/did inside"...when the call starts."
I would like to know + if / whetherfor yes/no questions"...whether the budget is approved."
Direct questionverb before subject"How is the testing going?"

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois do opener, use a ordem normal (sujeito + verbo) e NUNCA do/does/did dentro da pergunta. Errado: "Could you tell me where is the report?" Certo: "Could you tell me where the report is?" Para sim/não, use if ou whether (I would like to know whether the budget is approved).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Could you tell me how the testing going?"
"I would like to know the budget is approved."
"Let me start with a quick before the first item."
"That is a bit off-topic, so let me it and log a follow-up."
"I would rather not guess; let me it after the call."
"Let us with the follow-ups and the owners."

Stage 2: Build a Status Meeting in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of leading a status meeting in the correct order.

?You steer the discussion and keep people on track if they go off-topic.
?You open with a short summary, so everyone is on the same page.
?You close out, confirm the follow-ups and the owners, and end the meeting.
?You walk the team through the agenda, point by point.
?You handle questions: answer what you can, and double-check the rest later.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Could you tell me how the testing is going?
Você poderia me dizer como os testes estão indo?
I would like to know whether the budget is approved.
Eu gostaria de saber se o orçamento foi aprovado.
That is a bit off-topic, so let me defer it and log a follow-up.
Isso está um pouco fora do assunto, então vou adiar e registrar como uma tarefa.
I would rather not guess; let me double-check it after the call.
Prefiro não adivinhar; vou conferir isso depois da chamada.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to ask a senior leader politely about the timeline. Which is best?
A "Tell me when is the timeline ready."
B "Could you tell me when the timeline will be ready?"
C "When does the timeline is ready?"
As the moderator, someone raises an off-topic question. What is the best move?
A Spend the rest of the meeting on it.
B Defer it and log it as a follow-up, then return to the agenda.
C Tell the person their question is wrong.
Leadership asks for a number you do not have. What should you say?
A Make up a number so you look confident.
B "I would rather not guess; let me double-check it and get back to you."
C Stay silent and move on quickly.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are leading a status meeting for a real project at Microsoft. Open with a summary, then open two agenda items using indirect questions (Could you tell me how...? / I would like to know whether...?). Then handle one off-topic question (defer it as a follow-up) and close out with the next steps. Use new words like moderate, steer, summary, agenda item, defer and double-check.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Let me start with a quick summary, then we will go through the agenda.Vou começar com um resumo rápido e depois passamos pela pauta.
2Could you tell me how the testing is going?Você poderia me dizer como os testes estão indo?
3That is a bit off-topic, so let me defer it and log a follow-up.Isso está um pouco fora do assunto, então vou adiar e registrar como uma tarefa.
4I would rather not guess; let me double-check it and get back to you.Prefiro não adivinhar; vou conferir e dar retorno.
5Let us close out: here are the follow-ups and the owners.Vamos encerrar: aqui estão as tarefas e os responsáveis.
Aula 18 -- Pre-class

Persuading and Influencing -- Making a Recommendation

Make a clear recommendation and win support, using emphasis structures (not only... but also...; What we need is...). New vocabulary: recommend, make the case, win over, buy-in, advocate, endorse, sway, cost-effective, downside, weigh up.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recommend -- to say that an option is the best one to choose
"I would recommend the cloud option for the long term." (Recomendar)
Make the case -- to give clear reasons to support an idea
"Let me make the case for the new tool in two minutes." (Defender / argumentar a favor)
Win over -- to change someone's mind so they agree with you
"Her honest answer helped win over the whole board." (Conquistar / convencer)
Buy-in -- the agreement and support of the people involved
"We need buy-in from leadership before we start." (Apoio / adesão)
Advocate -- to publicly speak in favor of a plan or idea
"I will advocate for this plan in the next review." (Defender publicamente)
Endorse -- to officially support and approve a plan or person
"Leadership endorsed the new rollout plan." (Endossar / aprovar)
Sway -- to influence someone's opinion or decision
"One clear number can sway a whole meeting." (Influenciar)
Cost-effective -- giving good value for the money you spend
"The new tool is more cost-effective over a year." (Custo-benefício / econômico)
Downside -- the negative part or disadvantage of an option
"The one downside is the setup takes two weeks." (Desvantagem / ponto negativo)
Weigh up -- to compare the good and bad sides before deciding
"Let's weigh up both options before we choose." (Ponderar / avaliar)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Recommend
Make the case
Win over
Buy-in
Advocate
Endorse
Sway
Cost-effective
Downside
Weigh up

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie wants to recommend a new cloud tool. To win support, she will make the case clearly. She leads with emphasis: "What we need is a faster rollout." She explains that the tool is "not only faster, but also more cost-effective." Then she will weigh up both options out loud. To build trust, she names one honest downside: the setup takes two weeks. She believes this honesty will sway leadership and help her win them over. Finally, she asks for their buy-in directly, instead of just hoping they agree.

1. Which sentence emphasizes the main idea most strongly?
A "What we need is a faster rollout."
B "We need a faster rollout, maybe."
C "A rollout would be needed."
2. Why does Natalie name a downside herself?
A To weaken her own recommendation.
B To build trust and help win leadership over.
C Because she has no benefits to share.
3. How does Natalie end her case?
A She asks for buy-in directly.
B She just hopes everyone agrees.
C She ends without any ask.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Emphasis for Persuasion

GRAMMAR

Para persuadir, damos ênfase ao que importa. Use not only X, but also Y para unir duas ideias e dar peso às duas (X e Y na MESMA forma), e What we need is + substantivo para empurrar a ideia principal para a frente. / To persuade, we emphasize what matters. Use not only X, but also Y to join two points and give weight to both (X and Y in the SAME form), and What we need is + noun to push your main idea to the front.

StructureUseExample
not only X, but also Ygive weight to two points"...not only faster, but also cheaper."
X and Y same formkeep both sides balanced"not only recommend it, but also name the risk."
What we need is + nounpush the main idea to the front"What we need is a faster rollout."
Neutral sentenceless emphasis, plain order"We need a faster rollout."

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de not only, mantenha as duas partes na mesma forma (adjetivo + adjetivo, verbo + verbo). Errado: "not only faster, but also it is cheaper." Certo: "not only faster, but also cheaper." Use What we need is... para destacar a ideia central.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"It is not only faster, but also more ."
"What we need a faster rollout."
"Let me for the new tool."
"The one is the two-week setup."
"Honest answers help leadership."
"So, do I have your to move ahead?"

Stage 2: Build a Persuasive Pitch in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a persuasive recommendation in the correct order.

?You name one honest downside, because that builds trust and helps you win the room.
?You lead with the recommendation, so people hear your main idea right away.
?You ask for buy-in directly, instead of just hoping that everyone agrees.
?You give one strong reason and show the benefit.
?You handle objections calmly and weigh up the options out loud.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

What we need is a faster rollout, so I recommend the new tool.
O que precisamos é de um lançamento mais rápido, então recomendo a nova ferramenta.
It is not only faster, but also more cost-effective.
Não é só mais rápido, mas também mais econômico.
Honestly, the one downside is the setup time, but I would rather name it.
Sinceramente, a única desvantagem é o tempo de instalação, mas prefiro citar isso.
So, do I have your buy-in to move ahead?
Então, tenho o seu apoio para seguir em frente?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You want to open your recommendation with strong emphasis. Which is best?
A "Maybe we could think about a faster rollout someday."
B "What we need is a faster rollout, so I recommend the new tool."
C "A rollout, possibly, would be a thing."
Leadership thinks the idea is too expensive. What is the strongest reply?
A "It is not only faster, but also more cost-effective over a year."
B "It is faster, and also it is, you know, cheaper somehow."
C "Trust me, it is just better."
You want to build trust before asking for support. What should you do?
A Hide every risk so the plan looks perfect.
B Name one honest downside, then ask for their buy-in.
C Avoid asking for a decision at all.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are recommending a real idea to leadership at Microsoft. Lead with your recommendation using emphasis (What we need is...), give two benefits (not only... but also...), name one honest downside, and ask for buy-in at the end. Use new words like recommend, make the case, cost-effective, weigh up and win over.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1What we need is a faster rollout, so I recommend the new tool.O que precisamos é de um lançamento mais rápido, então recomendo a nova ferramenta.
2It is not only faster, but also more cost-effective.Não é só mais rápido, mas também mais econômico.
3Let me weigh up both options before we decide.Deixe-me ponderar as duas opções antes de decidirmos.
4Honestly, the one downside is the setup time, but I would rather name it.Sinceramente, a única desvantagem é o tempo de instalação, mas prefiro citá-la.
5So, do I have your buy-in to move ahead?Então, tenho o seu apoio para seguir em frente?
Aula 19 -- Pre-class

Negotiating with Confidence -- Standing Your Ground

Stand firm under pressure and reach a deal, using conditional connectors (as long as / provided that / unless). New vocabulary: stand firm, hold the line, pushback, justify, rationale, non-negotiable, assertive, leeway, firm, worthwhile.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Stand firm -- to keep your position and not change it under pressure
"We will stand firm on the deadline -- it cannot move." (Manter a posição)
Hold the line -- to refuse to change your position, especially on price
"I had to hold the line on the rate during the call." (Não ceder)
Pushback -- the resistance or objection you get from the other side
"We got strong pushback on the new terms." (Resistência / oposição)
Justify -- to give a good reason that supports a decision
"Let me justify the price with the long-term value." (Justificar)
Rationale -- the set of reasons that explain a decision
"The rationale for the rate is the extra support we provide." (Justificativa / razão)
Non-negotiable -- a point that is fixed and cannot be changed
"The delivery date is non-negotiable for us." (Inegociável)
Assertive -- confident and direct without being aggressive
"Stay assertive: calm, clear, and confident." (Assertivo / firme e calmo)
Leeway -- freedom or room to be flexible on a point
"We have some leeway on the timeline, but not on quality." (Margem / flexibilidade)
Firm -- strong, clear, and not easily changed
"My answer is firm: that price is final." (Firme)
Worthwhile -- worth the time, money, or effort it takes
"A fair deal makes the long talks worthwhile." (Que vale a pena)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Stand firm
Hold the line
Pushback
Justify
Rationale
Non-negotiable
Assertive
Leeway
Firm
Worthwhile

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The client gave Natalie strong pushback on the rate. She did not cave in. She decided to hold the line, but she stayed calm and assertive. First, she gave her rationale: the price covers extra support. Then she set a clear condition: "Unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate." She showed there was some leeway: "We can move on the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same." But quality was non-negotiable, so she had to stand firm. In the end, a longer term for a better rate made the deal worthwhile for both sides.

1. Which sentence states a negative condition correctly?
A "Unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate."
B "Unless we do not sign, we cannot lower the rate."
C "Unless we will sign, we cannot lower the rate."
2. Where did Natalie offer leeway?
A On the quality of the work.
B On the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same.
C On everything the client asked for.
3. What did Natalie do besides just refusing?
A She gave her rationale to justify her position.
B She raised her voice to win.
C She gave no reasons at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Conditional Connectors

GRAMMAR

Para negociar com firmeza sem parecer rígida, você define condições. Use as long as / provided that para dizer "somente se isto for verdade", e unless para dizer "se não". Depois do conector, use o presente (stays, sign), e na outra parte use can / will / cannot. / To stay firm without sounding rigid, you set conditions. Use as long as / provided that to say "only if this is true", and unless to say "if not". After the connector, use the present; in the other part, use can / will / cannot.

ConnectorMeaningExample
as long as + presentonly if this is true"...as long as the volume stays the same."
provided that + presentonly on this condition (formal)"Provided that you sign, we will hold the price."
unless + presentif NOT (the negative condition)"Unless we sign longer, we cannot lower the rate."
if + present (neutral)plain condition, less weight"We can lower it if you sign longer."

Key point / Ponto-chave: unless já significa "if not", então nunca use "not" de novo. Errado: "unless you do not sign." Certo: "unless you sign." E depois do conector use o presente, nunca "will": "as long as the volume stays", não "will stay".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We can move on the timeline, as long as the volume the same."
" we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate."
"Let me give you my for the rate."
"The delivery date is for us."
"I hear the pushback, but I have to on the rate."
"We have some on the smaller details."

Stage 2: Build a Confident Negotiation in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a confident negotiation in the correct order.

?You set a clear condition, using as long as or unless, to show where you can be flexible.
?You listen to the pushback calmly and do not cave in right away.
?You confirm the terms and make the deal worthwhile for both sides.
?You give your rationale, so your position never sounds like a flat no.
?You stand firm on the non-negotiable points, but offer some leeway on the smaller details.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

I hear the pushback, but I have to hold the line on the rate.
Eu entendo a resistência, mas preciso não ceder no valor.
We can move on the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same.
Podemos ceder no prazo, desde que o volume permaneça o mesmo.
Unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate.
A menos que assinemos um contrato mais longo, não podemos baixar o valor.
Quality is non-negotiable, so I have to stand firm on it.
A qualidade é inegociável, então preciso manter a posição nisso.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

The client pushes hard on price. What is the most confident reply?
A "Okay, fine, I will just lower it then."
B "I hear the pushback. My rationale is the extra support, so I have to hold the line."
C "No. That is final. Goodbye."
You want to lower the rate only if they commit to a longer contract. Which is best?
A "Unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate."
B "Unless we do not sign longer, we cannot lower the rate."
C "We lower the rate, no condition needed."
You want to show flexibility on the timeline but protect the volume. What should you say?
A "We can move on everything you want."
B "We can move on the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same."
C "The timeline is non-negotiable, sorry."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you are negotiating a real deal with a client at Microsoft. The client pushes back on the price. Hold the line, give your rationale, set one condition with "unless" or "as long as", stand firm on one non-negotiable point, and offer some leeway on the rest. Use new words like pushback, rationale, non-negotiable, leeway, assertive and worthwhile.

Survival Card -- Lesson 19

1I hear the pushback, but I have to hold the line on the rate.Eu entendo a resistência, mas preciso não ceder no valor.
2We can move on the timeline, as long as the volume stays the same.Podemos ceder no prazo, desde que o volume permaneça o mesmo.
3Unless we sign a longer contract, we cannot lower the rate.A menos que assinemos um contrato mais longo, não podemos baixar o valor.
4Quality is non-negotiable, so I have to stand firm on it.A qualidade é inegociável, então preciso manter a posição nisso.
5A fair deal makes the long talks worthwhile for both sides.Um acordo justo faz as longas conversas valerem a pena para os dois lados.
Aula 20 -- Pre-class

Reaching Agreement -- Confirming Terms & Closing Strongly

Confirm the terms and close the deal strongly, using the future perfect (will have + past participle) to project a finished result by a deadline. New vocabulary: confirm, restate, sum up, iron out, lock in, in writing, mutually beneficial, seal the deal, move forward, touch base.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Confirm -- to say clearly and officially that something is agreed or true
"Let me confirm the final price before we move on." (Confirmar)
Restate -- to say something again clearly to make sure it is understood
"Let me restate the terms so we are both clear." (Reafirmar / repetir)
Sum up -- to give a short, final summary of the main points
"To sum up, we agree on the price, the term, and the date." (Resumir)
Iron out -- to solve the last small problems or differences
"We just need to iron out the payment dates." (Resolver / acertar detalhes)
Lock in -- to make a price or term final so it cannot change
"Let's lock in the rate for the next two years." (Fixar / travar)
In writing -- written down on paper or by email, so it is official
"I'll put the agreed terms in writing this afternoon." (Por escrito)
Mutually beneficial -- good and fair for both sides of the agreement
"This is a mutually beneficial deal -- we both win." (Vantajoso para ambos)
Seal the deal -- to finish a negotiation and make the agreement official
"A handshake and a signature, and we seal the deal." (Fechar o acordo)
Move forward -- to start the next steps after an agreement is reached
"Now that we agree, let's move forward with the contract." (Avançar / seguir em frente)
Touch base -- to make a short contact later to check on progress
"Let's touch base next week to check the rollout." (Manter contato / dar um retorno)

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its translation.

Confirm
Restate
Sum up
Iron out
Lock in
In writing
Mutually beneficial
Seal the deal
Move forward
Touch base

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Natalie and the client finally reached an agreement. To close it cleanly, she decided to restate every term and confirm it, so nothing was left open. They locked in the rate for two years, and only had to iron out the payment dates. Then Natalie projected the finished result with the future perfect: "By tonight, I will have sent the terms in writing, and by Friday, we will have signed the contract." The client agreed it was a mutually beneficial deal, so they were happy to seal the deal, move forward with the rollout, and touch base the following week.

1. Which sentence uses the future perfect correctly?
A "By Friday, we will have signed the contract."
B "By Friday, we will have sign the contract."
C "By Friday, we will signed the contract."
2. What did Natalie do to make sure nothing was left open?
A She ended the call quickly.
B She restated and confirmed every term, then put it in writing.
C She changed the rate at the last minute.
3. Why were both sides happy to seal the deal?
A Because it was mutually beneficial -- good for both sides.
B Because only Natalie won.
C Because nothing was agreed at all.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Future Perfect

GRAMMAR

Para fechar com confiança, projete o resultado pronto: antes de um marco no futuro, a tarefa estará concluída. Use o future perfect: will have + particípio passado (signed, sent, agreed). Sempre acompanhe de um marco com by: by Friday, by then, by the end of the call. / To close with confidence, project the finished result: by a future point, the task will be done. Use the future perfect: will have + past participle. Always pair it with a deadline using by.

FormPatternExample
Affirmativewill have + past participle"By Friday, we will have signed the contract."
Negativewon't have + past participle"By then, we won't have finished the paperwork."
QuestionWill + subject + have + p.p.?"Will we have agreed on everything by Friday?"
Time markerby + future point"by Friday, by then, by the end of the call"

Key point / Ponto-chave: depois de will have use o particípio passado (signed, sent), nunca o verbo base. Errado: "we will have sign." Certo: "we will have signed." E use by (não "until") para o prazo de conclusão.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word or form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"By Friday, we will have the contract."
"Let me the terms to confirm them."
"By tonight, I will have the terms in writing."
"This is a deal, so let's seal it."
"We just need to the payment dates."
"Now that we agree, let's with the contract."

Stage 2: Build a Strong Close in Order

Order

Listen first, then put the steps of a strong close in the correct order.

?You lock in what is agreed and iron out the last small details.
?You sum up the discussion so both sides are on the same page.
?You put everything in writing, seal the deal, and move forward.
?You restate each term and confirm it out loud, so nothing is left open.
?You project the finished result with the future perfect: "By Friday, we will have signed the contract."

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each phrase, then record yourself saying it.

Let me restate the terms to confirm them.
Deixe-me reafirmar os termos para confirmá-los.
By Friday, we will have signed the contract.
Até sexta-feira, teremos assinado o contrato.
By tonight, I will have sent the terms in writing.
Até hoje à noite, terei enviado os termos por escrito.
This is a mutually beneficial deal, so let's seal it.
Este é um acordo vantajoso para ambos, então vamos fechá-lo.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best sentence for each situation.

You and the client just agreed. How do you confirm it cleanly?
A "Okay, I think we're done, bye."
B "Let me restate the terms to confirm them, and I'll put them in writing."
C "We'll figure out the details some other time."
The client asks when the contract will be ready. Which is the best, most confident reply?
A "By Friday, we will have signed the contract."
B "By Friday, we will have sign the contract."
C "Maybe sometime, I'm not sure."
You want to close strongly and say what happens next. What should you say?
A "Let's wait and see if it works out."
B "Let's seal the deal, move forward with the rollout, and I'll touch base next week."
C "I'll think about whether to continue."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine you have just reached an agreement on a real deal at Microsoft. Close it strongly: sum up and restate the terms, confirm them, lock in what is agreed, iron out any last details, project the finished result with the future perfect ("By Friday, we will have signed..."), put it in writing, and seal the deal. Use new words like confirm, restate, lock in, in writing, mutually beneficial, seal the deal and move forward.

Survival Card -- Lesson 20

1Let me restate the terms to confirm them.Deixe-me reafirmar os termos para confirmá-los.
2By tonight, I will have sent the terms in writing.Até hoje à noite, terei enviado os termos por escrito.
3By Friday, we will have signed the contract.Até sexta-feira, teremos assinado o contrato.
4This is a mutually beneficial deal, so let's seal it.Este é um acordo vantajoso para ambos, então vamos fechá-lo.
5Let's move forward, and I'll touch base next week.Vamos seguir em frente, e darei um retorno na próxima semana.

IN CLASS -- Aulas Disponíveis

Selecione a aula para iniciar os slides interativos.

What Are You Working On?

The Art of Meetings
Modal verbs -- 37 slides
06
Writing Business Reports

Procurement & Tenders -- Winning the Bid
First conditional + polite requests -- 27 slides
08
Presenting to Leadership -- The Elevator Pitch

09
Agreeing and Disagreeing -- Professional Debates
Linking words for contrast -- 27 slides
10
Handling Difficult Conversations -- Giving and Receiving Feedback
