11
Building Trust -- Networking and Professional Relationships
Question tags -- 27 slides
12
Midpoint Review -- Integration Check
Review of structures 1-11 -- 27 slides
13
Negotiation Basics -- Finding Common Ground
Second conditional + negotiation frames -- 27 slides
14
Closing the Deal -- Confirming Agreements
Reported speech -- 27 slides
15
Project Kick-off -- Giving Updates
Future continuous -- 27 slides
16
Project Updates -- Reporting Progress
Present perfect continuous -- 27 slides
17
Leading the Status Meeting -- Moderating & Handling Questions
Indirect questions -- 27 slides
18
Persuading and Influencing -- Making a Recommendation
Emphasis for persuasion -- 27 slides
19
Negotiating with Confidence -- Standing Your Ground
Conditional connectors -- 27 slides
20
Reaching Agreement -- Confirming Terms & Closing Strongly
Future perfect -- 27 slides

Supplementary Materials

Lesson 1 — Who Is Natalie Viegas?

Series
Succession (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1

Corporate vocabulary in real context. Observe how executives introduce themselves, negotiate, and lead meetings in English. Directly related to your day-to-day at Microsoft.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note 5 business expressions.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz — LinkedIn: Reid Hoffman

Reid Hoffman shares his professional journey: early failed attempts at social networking, founding LinkedIn as a jobs-and-careers platform, and its $26 billion sale to Microsoft in 2016.

Focus on corporate vocabulary (network, platform, acquisition, career) and pause to repeat key business phrases aloud.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
Microsoft Ignite Keynote -- Satya Nadella

Real Microsoft presentations. Observe the technology, cloud computing, and digital transformation vocabulary used by the CEO. Directly relevant to your work.

Tip: watch 10 minutes and identify vocabulary words from this lesson.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Your Monday at Microsoft

Series
The Bear (FX/Hulu) -- Season 1, Episode 1

Fast-paced American English. Observe how the characters describe routines under pressure, delegate tasks, and use daily operations vocabulary.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note how they describe their routines.

Disponível no streaming: Disney+

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — The Real Reason You Procrastinate

Adam Grant interviews high performers (including author Margaret Atwood) about how they organize their work, beat procrastination, and stay productive. The twist: procrastination is about managing emotions, not laziness.

Listen for the practical routines guests describe for prioritizing tasks and getting started.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Microsoft 365 Productivity Tips

Real productivity tips with Microsoft tools. Professional routine vocabulary, daily workflows, and task organization in English.

Tip: watch 10 minutes and identify vocabulary words from this lesson.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — Career Story: From Rio to São Paulo

Series
Halt and Catch Fire (Netflix) -- Season 1, Episode 1

Career journeys in the technology industry. Observe how the characters describe their professional paths, city moves, and career transitions in English.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note how the characters talk about their careers using Past Simple.

Disponível no streaming: Prime Video

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — How to Make Better Decisions Under Pressure

A conversation on how leaders make hard calls: taking an expansive view of a situation, weighing pros and cons and trade-offs, and reaching a decision instead of falling back on habit. Rich input for the language of choices and reasoning.

Listen for how speakers compare options ('on one hand… on the other hand').

Open episode ↗

YouTube
TED Talk: "The Puzzle of Motivation" -- Dan Pink

Reflection on career and professional motivation. Dan Pink uses Past Simple to tell stories and examples. Excellent for practicing past narrative listening.

Tip: watch 10 minutes and identify the Past Simple verbs and time expressions.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — What Are You Working On?

Series
Silicon Valley (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Minimum Viable Product"

Tech startup culture and project pitches. Observe how the characters describe what they are working on, present deliverables, and talk about scalability. Vocabulary directly related to your project management context.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note every time someone uses Present Continuous to describe current work.

Disponível no streaming: Max

Podcast
Masters of Scale — Airbnb's Brian Chesky: Do Things That Don't Scale

Reid Hoffman and Airbnb's Brian Chesky tell the story of how Airbnb grew from hand-crafted, unscalable beginnings into a global business, and what leaders accomplish along the way.

Listen for present perfect ('we've built…') and present continuous ('we're working on…') as leaders describe their work.

Listen on Spotify ↗

YouTube
TED Talk: "How Great Leaders Inspire Action" -- Simon Sinek

Leadership and project communication. Simon Sinek uses Present Perfect and Present Continuous naturally throughout the talk. Observe how he describes ongoing processes and accomplished results.

Tip: watch 10 minutes and identify how the speaker uses "have done" vs "are doing" to describe progress.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — Running & Chairing Effective Meetings

Series
The Morning Show (Apple TV+) -- Season 1, Episode 4

Watch how the news team chairs editorial meetings, sets agendas, and reaches consensus under pressure. Notice the vocabulary for facilitating, recapping action items, and adjourning discussions.

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note how characters transition between agenda items using phrases like "Let us move to..." and "To recap..."

Disponível no streaming: Apple TV+

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — Why Meetings Go Wrong (And How to Fix Them)

UNC's Steven Rogelberg explains why so much meeting time is wasted and how to fix it: question-based agendas, fewer attendees, and better follow-through and decisions.

Collect the meeting vocabulary: agenda, objectives, action items, consensus.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Succession — Official Trailer

Trailer oficial da HBO com diálogos rápidos e naturais em inglês corporativo, ótimo para treinar registro formal e vocabulário de negócios.

Assista com legendas em inglês e repita as falas dos personagens para praticar entonação.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — Writing & Presenting Professional Reports

Series
Suits -- Season 2, Episode 5: "Break Point"

Watch how the legal team drafts, revises, and presents findings in high-stakes reports. Notice the passive voice structures used in formal business communication: "The report was reviewed...", "Recommendations have been made..."

Tip: watch with English subtitles and note every passive voice sentence the characters use when discussing reports and filings.

Disponível nos streamings: Netflix e Prime Video

Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant — How to Love Criticism

Adam Grant visits a workplace built on radical candor to learn how professionals give, receive, and act on tough feedback. The key idea is the 'second score': you're judged less on the feedback itself than on how you respond and revise.

Notice how guests draft, revise, and reframe their reaction to negative feedback.

Open episode ↗

YouTube
Succession — Official Trailer

Trailer oficial da HBO com diálogos rápidos e naturais em inglês corporativo, ótimo para treinar registro formal e vocabulário de negócios.

Assista com legendas em inglês e repita as falas dos personagens para praticar entonação.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — Procurement & Tenders

YouTube
What is Procurement? (Supply Chain Basics)

Watch a short, clear video about how procurement works: tenders, bids, vendors and approval. Listen for the words you learned today and notice how buyers and suppliers talk about a deal.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com tender e uma com bid.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
A review of conditionals: BBC English Class

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about "If + present, will + verb" and polite ways to ask for things at work (Could you...? / Would you mind + -ing?). Perfect to review today's grammar.

Dica: escreva três frases sobre o seu trabalho -- uma com o first conditional, uma com could you e uma com would you mind.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Savory Snacks: Mars Inc.'s acquisition of Kellanova | 339

The hosts cover Mars Inc.'s multibillion-dollar acquisition of snack maker Kellanova, walking through the deal, its terms and the strategic reasons behind it.

Note deal-and-contract vocabulary (acquire, terms, value, close) as the hosts describe the transaction.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 8 — Presenting to Leadership

YouTube
The Perfect Elevator Pitch - Best Examples and Templates

Watch a short, clear video about building a two-minute pitch: value proposition, one strong number and a single takeaway. Notice how speakers use the present perfect to share results.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com value proposition e uma com takeaway.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
The present perfect with 'just', 'already' and 'yet' - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about "have/has + past participle" for results, and quantifiers (many, much, a lot of, several, plenty of). Perfect to review today's grammar.

Dica: escreva três frases sobre o seu trabalho -- duas com o present perfect e uma com um quantificador.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Think Fast, Talk Smart — 227. Tech Tools: Move Your Audience By Moving Through Your Presentation

Prezi CEO Jim Szafranski and Matt Abrahams discuss how to present and pitch ideas visually, moving an audience through your story instead of reading slide by slide.

Listen for how they keep an audience engaged, then plan one 'move' for your next presentation.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 9 — Agreeing and Disagreeing

YouTube
"How to Disagree Respectfully" -- TED-Ed / Harvard Business Review

Watch a short, clear video about disagreeing well at work: concede valid points, give your objection with a reason, and look for common ground. Notice the linking words speakers use to show contrast.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com however e uma com although.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Practice
Grammar: Linking words of contrast - BBC English Masterclass

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about linking words for contrast and concession. Perfect to review when to use however (two sentences) and although (start of a clause).

Dica: escreva três frases sobre uma decisão no trabalho -- uma com however, uma com although e uma com on the other hand.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — The Great Kiwi Exodus | 408

The hosts debate why so many New Zealanders are leaving the country and the workforce challenges it creates — a discussion full of differing viewpoints.

Listen for how the hosts agree, disagree and build on each other's points during the discussion.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 10 — Handling Difficult Conversations

YouTube
How to Give Feedback—Especially When You're Dreading it | Christine vs. Work

A short, clear video on giving constructive feedback and receiving it without being defensive: start positive, address one issue, and reassure the person. Notice how speakers stay calm and tactful.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase com "He said that..." e uma com "She told me to...".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
English Class: Reported speech

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about reported speech. Perfect to review when to use say (no person), tell (a person), and tell / ask someone to do, plus how the verb moves one step back in time.

Dica: pegue três frases reais do seu chefe e reescreva-as: uma com said that, uma com told me that e uma com asked me to.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
HBR IdeaCast — The Secret to Giving Great Feedback

Kim Scott, co-founder of Radical Candor, on giving honest feedback people can actually receive: care personally while challenging directly, and how professionals should take criticism.

Write down the softening phrases managers use before delivering hard feedback.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 11 — Building Trust

YouTube
How To Hack Networking | David Burkus | TEDxUniversityofNevada

A short, clear video on building genuine professional relationships: how to start a conversation, find a mutual interest, and follow up to build trust and credibility over time.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases com question tags (por exemplo, "..., don't you?" e "..., haven't we?").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
Question tags - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about question tags. Perfect to review how a positive sentence takes a negative tag, how the tag matches the auxiliary (be, have, do), and how to use tags to confirm and connect.

Dica: pegue cinco frases sobre seus contatos e adicione um question tag a cada uma; confira o auxiliar e o pronome.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
All Ears English — AEE: Why Relationships Determine Your Career Success

A real All Ears English episode on why building genuine professional relationships drives career success, with practical mindset and language for connecting with people at work.

Note one relationship-building idea and act on it this week — send a friendly follow-up message to a contact.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 12 — Midpoint Review

YouTube
How to Give a Project Update in English at Work

A short video on reporting progress to leadership: how to summarize results, compare periods, and confirm next steps. Perfect input to review the present perfect, comparatives, and the language of a midpoint update.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases no present perfect ("We have...") e uma com comparativo ("...stronger than...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
Present and past passives - 6 Minute Grammar

Short lessons and quizzes that review the core structures of your first semester: the present perfect for experience, the passive voice for reports, and the first conditional for plans. Great to consolidate everything in one place.

Dica: escreva cinco frases sobre seu trabalho na Microsoft -- uma para cada estrutura do "structure map" -- e confira o past participle e o "than" dos comparativos.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Beauty in Distress: What went wrong at The Body Shop? | 313

The hosts review the rise and fall of The Body Shop, tracing the company's progress, setbacks and the decisions that led to its troubles.

Treat it like a project review — note the phrases used to report what went well and what went wrong.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 13 — Negotiation Basics

YouTube
The Best Way to Win a Negotiation, According to a Harvard Business Professor | Inc.

A short video on principled negotiation: how to look past the price, discover the other side's priorities, and propose a trade-off that works for both sides. Perfect input to review leverage, bottom line, concession, and common ground in real context.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote uma frase no segundo condicional ("If they..., I would...") e dois termos novos de negociação.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
BBC Learning English -- The Second Conditional

Short lessons and quizzes on the second conditional: the form (if + past simple, would + base verb), the use for imaginary situations, and the special case "If I were you". Great to consolidate the grammar of this lesson.

Dica: escreva cinco frases sobre uma negociação na Microsoft -- todas no segundo condicional -- e confira que você nunca usou "would" depois de "if".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — The Netflix–Warner Bros. Acquisition (Part 1) | 400

The hosts explain how Netflix won a bidding battle to acquire major Warner Bros. Discovery assets — a real-world look at competitive deal-making.

Notice the language of offers and counteroffers as the hosts describe the bidding war.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 14 — Closing the Deal

YouTube
How To Close a Deal In Sales - 3 Powerful Closing Techniques To Closing a Sales Deal

A short, clear video on closing a deal professionally: how to confirm the agreed terms, make sure both sides are on the same page, and finalize the paperwork without losing the relationship.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases que confirmam um acordo (por exemplo, "Let me confirm the terms..." e "Once we sign off...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
BBC Learning English -- Reported Speech

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about reported speech. Perfect to review how the verb shifts back one tense (is→was, will→would, can→could) and the difference between said and told + person.

Dica: pegue cinco frases que alguém disse hoje e reporte cada uma; confira o backshift e se você usou said ou told corretamente.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — BuzzFeed's Hot Sale of Hot Ones | 351

The hosts cover why BuzzFeed sold the production company behind Hot Ones — the financial pressure behind the sale and how the deal was closed.

Listen for phrases used to describe a completed sale and a finalized agreement.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 15 — Project Kick-off

YouTube
Project Kickoff Meeting Guide | WHAT TO INCLUDE AND HOW TO RUN THE MEETING!

A short, clear video on running a kick-off: how to present the timeline, assign owners to each workstream, and align the whole team on what happens next.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases no future continuous (por exemplo, "Next week we will be..." e "I will be leading...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
BBC Learning English -- The Future Continuous

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about the future continuous. Perfect to review the form will be + verb-ing and when to use it for actions in progress at a future time.

Dica: descreva cinco coisas que você estará fazendo na próxima semana usando "I will be ...-ing"; confira se você manteve o "be".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Tokenmaxxing and the Corporate AI Pullback | 414

The hosts explain how runaway AI token costs are forcing companies to rein in their AI projects and rethink budgets and rollout plans.

Note how the hosts describe project scope, costs and status as they discuss corporate AI plans.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 16 — Project Updates

YouTube
HOW should I talk to Executive Leadership about Project Status

A short, clear video on reporting progress to leadership: how to lead with a headline, show the metrics, explain ongoing work, and flag a blocker honestly without losing trust.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases com o present perfect continuous (por exemplo, "We have been working..." e "The team has been testing...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
BBC Learning English -- Present Perfect Continuous

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about the present perfect continuous. Perfect to review have/has been + -ing for ongoing actions, and the difference between for (a period) and since (a start point).

Dica: escreva cinco frases sobre seu projeto atual usando for e since; depois transforme duas em present perfect simples (have completed) para comparar processo e resultado.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Understanding India's Economic Growth | 268

The hosts give a status report on India's economy — its demographics, current health and where the numbers are heading.

Listen for progress-and-status language (growth, on track, ahead, behind) and note how figures are reported.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 17 — Leading the Status Meeting

YouTube
How to Run an Effective Meeting 5 Tips

A short, clear video on leading a meeting: how to open with a summary, walk through the agenda, keep people on track, handle questions, and close out with follow-ups and owners.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas perguntas indiretas que você poderia usar para conduzir uma reunião ("Could you tell me..." e "I would like to know whether...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
Indirect Questions - 6 Minute Grammar

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about indirect questions. Perfect to review the openers (Could you tell me..., Do you know..., I would like to know...), the normal word order after them, and if / whether for yes/no questions.

Dica: pegue cinco perguntas diretas do seu trabalho e transforme cada uma em pergunta indireta, lembrando de tirar o do/does/did e de manter sujeito + verbo.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Sneakernomics Revisited | 410

The hosts lead a structured discussion of how the sportswear market has shifted, weighing Nike and Adidas against new challengers.

Notice how the hosts steer the conversation and field each other's questions — useful for leading a meeting.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 18 — Persuading and Influencing

YouTube
How to Influence Without Authority at Work [Persuasion - Part 1 of 3]

A short, practical video on persuading and influencing at work: how to lead with your recommendation, frame benefits, name the downside honestly, and win buy-in from people who do not report to you.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas frases de ênfase que você poderia usar para fazer uma recomendação ("What we need is..." e "not only... but also...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
Grammar: Giving emphasis in English - BBC English Masterclass

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about emphasis structures. Perfect to review "not only X, but also Y" (with both parts in the same form) and front-focus patterns like "What we need is...".

Dica: pegue cinco frases neutras do seu trabalho e reescreva cada uma com ênfase, lembrando de manter a mesma forma dos dois lados depois de "not only".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Business English from All Ears English — BE 294: Buy Time, Get Confidence with These English Phrases

A free Business English episode with go-to phrases to stay composed, weigh options and make your case confidently in meetings - useful input for recommending ideas and winning support.

Listen once, then note two phrases you could use to buy a moment before making a recommendation.

Listen on Spotify ↗

Lesson 19 — Negotiating with Confidence

YouTube
Negotiate in English with Confidence and Fluency: Top Phrases & Strategies for Business English

A short, practical video on standing your ground in a negotiation: how to handle pushback calmly, hold the line, give your rationale, and stay assertive without being aggressive.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote duas condições que você poderia definir numa negociação ("Unless..." e "As long as...").

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
Inverted Conditionals & Even if, Only if, Unless - English Grammar with JenniferESL

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about conditional connectors. Perfect to review how to set conditions: "unless" (if not), "as long as" and "provided that", always with the present tense after the connector.

Dica: pegue cinco condições do seu trabalho e escreva cada uma com "unless" e depois com "as long as", lembrando de usar o presente após o conector.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Steel Standoff: The Blocked Nippon-US Steel Acquisition | 355

The hosts break down why the US blocked Nippon Steel's $14.9B bid for US Steel — a standoff over foreign investment and national security where neither side backed down.

Listen for firm, assertive language used to set conditions and hold a position.

Open episode ↗

Lesson 20 — Reaching Agreement & Closing Strongly

YouTube
The Most POWERFUL Sales Closing Technique of All-Time

A short, practical video on closing a negotiation cleanly: how to restate and confirm the terms, put everything in writing, and seal a mutually beneficial deal with confidence.

Dica: assista com legenda em inglês e anote três frases que você usaria para confirmar e fechar um acordo no seu trabalho.

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Practice
The future perfect - BBC English Class

Short, clear lessons and quizzes about the future perfect. Perfect to review how to project a finished result before a deadline: "By Friday, we will have signed everything", always with a time marker using "by".

Dica: escreva cinco metas de trabalho com o future perfect ("By next month, I will have..."), sempre com um marco usando "by".

Assistir no YouTube ↗

Podcast
Down to Business English — Savory Snacks: Mars Inc.'s Acquisition of Kellanova

The hosts break down Mars Inc.'s multi-billion-dollar acquisition of Kellanova — a real deal being closed — covering the terms, the strategy, and the vocabulary of mergers and acquisitions. Great B1-B2 listening input on how a deal actually comes together.

Note every phrase used to describe the deal terms and write down 5 M&A words you hear (acquisition, stake, buyout, etc.).

Open episode ↗

The Dream Key Words The Code In Context Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
Lesson 1 -- Business English

Who Is
Natalie Viegas?

B1+ -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Let's Talk About You

"Tell me about your role at Microsoft."
"What kind of clients do you work with?"
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your English Goals

"What are you working on at Microsoft right now?"
"When do you need English the most at work?"
"What would change in your career if your English were stronger?"
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Your Executive
Vocabulary

Ten essential words for describing your role

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

To watch over and manage a process or team
Oversee
To watch over and manage a process or team
"I oversee the entire public sector portfolio."
To be in charge of a team or project
Lead
To be in charge of a team or project
"She leads the government sales division."
To deal with or manage a situation or task
Handle
To deal with or manage a situation or task
"We handle procurement for public institutions."
To communicate and cooperate between groups
Liaise
To communicate and cooperate between groups
"I liaise between engineering and the client."
Click each card to reveal
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To be accountable to a manager or superior
Report to
To be accountable to a manager or superior
"I report to the VP every Monday."
To have the duty of managing something
Be responsible for
To have the duty of managing something
"I am responsible for revenue targets."
A person with interest or influence in a project
Stakeholder
A person with interest or influence in a project
"The key stakeholders approved the deal."
A set of potential deals or projects in progress
Pipeline
A set of potential deals or projects in progress
"Our pipeline is growing this quarter."
Click each card to reveal
Review

Say It All

Click each word to listen. Repeat each one twice.

Oversee"I oversee the portfolio."
Lead"She leads the team."
Handle"We handle procurement."
Liaise"I liaise with legal."
Report to"I report to the VP."
Be responsible for"I am responsible for targets."
Stakeholder"The stakeholders approved it."
Pipeline"The pipeline is growing."
Currently"I am currently leading a project."
Drive"We drive innovation."
Speaking

Use the Words

1. Describe what you manage at Microsoft.
"I oversee public sector accounts for courts and ministries."
2. Explain your reporting structure.
"I report to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America."
3. Tell me about a current project.
"I am currently driving a digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice."
Chapter 3: The Code

The Grammar
Behind Your Role

Present Simple vs Present Continuous

Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the highlighted words. What pattern do you see?

She oversees government accounts. She reports to the VP.
She is currently working on a deal with the Ministry.
Natalie handles procurement processes every week.
Her team is driving a transformation project right now.
TenseUseExample
Present SimpleFacts / RoutinesShe oversees accounts.
Present SimplePermanent situationsShe reports to the VP.
Present ContinuousCurrent projectShe is working on a deal.
Present ContinuousTemporary actionThey are driving a project.
Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I am work at Microsoft."
"I work at Microsoft." (permanent fact)
"I work on a new project this week."
"I am working on a new project this week." (temporary)
Practice

Fill in: Simple or Continuous?

"She ___oversees government accounts every day."
"Right now, she ___is working on a new deal."
"Natalie ___reports to the VP of Public Sector."
"This quarter, her team ___is driving a transformation project."
"She ___handles procurement processes regularly."
"I ___am currently leading the proposal phase."
Practice

Your Turn: About You

Present Simple (routines/facts):

Tell me 2 things you do every day or every week at Microsoft.

Present Continuous (current/temporary):

Tell me 2 things you are working on right now or this month.

Practice

Simple or Continuous?

"I ___ (lead) the public sector team." ?lead (permanent role = Simple)
"We ___ (negotiate) a new contract this week." ?are negotiating (this week = Continuous)
"She ___ (liaise) with three departments." ?liaises (general fact = Simple)
"The pipeline ___ (grow) at the moment." ?is growing (at the moment = Continuous)
Chapter 4: Getting There

Natalie at the
Global Summit

Time to use your vocabulary in a real professional situation.

Artifact

Your Business Card

NV
Natalie Viegas
Account Executive -- Public Sector

Company: Microsoft Brasil

Division: Public Sector Sales -- Courts & Public Ministries

Reports to: VP of Public Sector Sales, Latin America

Location: São Paulo, SP

Focus: Digital Transformation & Cloud Solutions

Dialogue

Meeting VP James Chen

J
Hi, I am James Chen, VP of Public Sector for Asia Pacific. Are you from Microsoft Brasil?
N
Yes! I am Natalie Viegas. I am an Account Executive in the public sector team.
J
Great to meet you! What is your role there?
N
I oversee government accounts. I handle deals with courts and public ministries.
J
Interesting! What are you currently working on?
N
I am currently driving a major digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice. We are building a cloud-based case management system.
J
That sounds impressive! Who do you report to?
N
I report to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America. I also liaise with our global solutions team to ensure we meet stakeholder expectations.
Comprehension

About James Chen

1. What is James Chen's role?
VP of Public Sector for Asia Pacific.
2. What does James want to know about Natalie?
Her role, current projects, and reporting structure.
3. What impressed James about Natalie's work?
The digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice.
Listening

Conference Introduction

Listen to an executive introducing herself at a global summit.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's name and role?
Natalie Viegas, Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil
2. What does she oversee?
Public sector accounts, working with courts and public ministries
3. What is she currently driving?
A digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice
Comprehension

What Did You Hear?

1. What region does the speaker cover?
The southeast region of Brazil.
2. What is growing in her portfolio?
The pipeline of government contracts.
3. What is she responsible for closing?
Enterprise deals across the southeast region.
Chapter 5: Practice

Time to
Practice

Listening, quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Listening

Team Meeting

Listen to an executive giving a project update at a team meeting.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What phase is the Ministry of Justice project in?
The proposal phase
2. Who does the speaker report to for this project?
VP Martinez
Comprehension

Team Meeting Review

1. What is the next step in the project?
Handle the security compliance review.
2. How does the speaker describe the pipeline?
The pipeline looks strong for Q3.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A VP at a global summit asks: "What do you do at Microsoft?" Introduce yourself professionally.
"I am an Account Executive at Microsoft Brasil. I oversee public sector accounts for courts and ministries."
Detective

Spot the Error

"She collaborate with many departments."
"She collaborates with many departments."
"I am currently work on a new project."
"I am currently working on a new project."
"He lead the team since 2020."
"He has led the team since 2020."
"I am responsible of the sales targets."
"I am responsible for the sales targets."
0 / 4 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. A colleague asks what you oversee. Answer.
"I oversee public sector accounts for courts and ministries."
2. Your VP asks what you are currently working on. Respond.
"I am currently driving a digital transformation project with the Ministry of Justice."
3. A new team member asks who you report to. Answer.
"I report to the VP of Public Sector Sales for Latin America."
4. Describe your pipeline status this quarter.
"Our pipeline is growing steadily this quarter. We have several strong deals in progress."
5. Explain what your team drives.
"Our team drives digital transformation in the public sector through cloud-based solutions."
6. Tell a stakeholder what you are responsible for.
"I am responsible for closing enterprise deals with public institutions across the southeast."
Challenge

5-Word Challenge

Use at least 5 vocabulary words from today to describe your role at Microsoft in one paragraph.

overseeleadhandleliaisereport toresponsible forstakeholderpipelinecurrentlydrive
Chapter 6: Your Turn

From Guided
to Free

Show what you have learned today

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Summit Lobby

You are at a Microsoft Global Summit. VP James Chen from Asia Pacific approaches you. Introduce yourself, describe your role, explain what you are currently working on, and mention your reporting structure.
overseehandlecurrentlydrivereport to
Role-Play

Role-Play: Team Introduction

A new colleague from the US just joined the Latin America team. They ask: "Tell me about your work." Describe your responsibilities, your current projects, and your stakeholders.
responsible forstakeholderpipeline
Free Practice

Role-Play: Free Conversation

You are at the conference dinner. A senior executive from another region asks about your career and what excites you about your work. Have a free conversation. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use everything you learned today: your role, responsibilities, current projects, and professional vocabulary.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Introduce yourself as if you are at a Microsoft Global Summit. Include: your name, your role, what you oversee, what you are currently working on, and who you report to.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Almost Done!

Delayed error correction, survival phrases, and self-assessment

Error Correction

Let's Polish

Share 3-4 errors you noticed during the class. Use the format below:

What you said What to say instead

Write the errors on this slide or say them orally. This is a safe space for learning.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"Let me introduce myself. I am Natalie Viegas from Microsoft Brasil."
2
"I oversee public sector sales for courts and ministries."
3
"I am currently working on a cloud solution for the Ministry of Justice."
4
"I report to the VP of Public Sector for Latin America."
5
"I would love to discuss how our teams can collaborate."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can introduce myself professionally in English.
I know 10 professional vocabulary words for my role.
I can use Present Simple for facts and routines.
I can use Present Continuous for current projects.
I can describe my role to a VP at a global summit.
I can explain my reporting structure in English.
I can talk about my pipeline and stakeholders.
I feel more confident about speaking English at work.
Lesson Complete

Day 1 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Your Monday at Microsoft -- Routines and Daily Operations

Lesson 2 -- Business English

Natalie's Monday
at Microsoft

B1+ -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: The Dream

Quick Review

From last class: can you still use these words?

"What do you oversee at Microsoft?"
"Who do you report to, and what is in your pipeline right now?"
"Tell me one thing you handle every week."
Chapter 1: The Dream

Your Monday

"What is the first thing you do when you arrive at the office on Monday?"
"How do you organize your week?"
"Do you have any meetings you never miss?"
Chapter 2: Packing Words

Your Routine
Vocabulary

Ten essential words for describing your daily operations

Vocabulary

Words 1-4

A plan that lists times for events or activities
Schedule
A plan that lists times for events or activities
"I check my schedule every morning."
To travel regularly between home and work
Commute
To travel regularly between home and work
"I commute from Vila Madalena to Itaim Bibi."
To be present at an event or meeting
Attend
To be present at an event or meeting
"I attend three client meetings a week."
To give someone the key information about something
Brief
To give someone the key information
"I brief my team on the pipeline every Monday."
Click each card to reveal
Vocabulary

Words 5-8

To examine or look at something carefully
Review
To examine or look at something carefully
"I always review contracts before a call."
To decide which tasks are the most important
Prioritize
To decide which tasks are the most important
"I prioritize urgent client requests."
To take further action after initial contact
Follow up
To take further action after initial contact
"I need to follow up with the Ministry."
The latest time by which something must be done
Deadline
The latest time by which something must be done
"The deadline is next Friday."
Click each card to reveal
Vocabulary

Words 9-10

To spend time finding out what has happened
Catch up
To spend time finding out what has happened
"I usually catch up with my team at nine."
To finish or complete something
Wrap up
To finish or complete something
"Let me wrap up this presentation."
Click each card to reveal
Speaking

Use the Words

1. Describe how you start your Monday morning.
"I check my schedule and review my urgent emails first."
2. Tell me about your commute.
"I commute from Vila Madalena to the Microsoft office in Itaim Bibi. It takes about forty minutes."
3. What meetings do you attend on Mondays?
"I attend the leadership meeting at nine and then I brief my team on the week's priorities."
Chapter 3: The Code

The Grammar of
Routines

Present Simple + Adverbs of Frequency

Chapter 3: The Code

Grammar Discovery

Look at the highlighted words. What do they have in common?

I always check my schedule in the morning.
She usually attends the nine AM meeting.
He often briefs his team after lunch.
I never skip my Monday morning routine.
AdverbFrequencyPosition
always100%Before main verb
usually~80%Before main verb
often~60%Before main verb
sometimes~40%Before main verb / Start of sentence
rarely~10%Before main verb
never0%Before main verb
Position Rule

Where Does the Adverb Go?

Before the main verb:

"I always check my schedule."

"She usually attends meetings."

"We never skip the briefing."

After the verb BE:

"She is usually on time."

"He is always busy on Mondays."

"I am rarely late."

Common Mistake

Watch Out!

"I check always my email."
"I always check my email."
"He attend never the meeting."
"He never attends the meeting."
Practice

Place the Adverb

"I ___ review my emails in the morning." (always) ?I always review my emails in the morning.
"She ___ attends the Monday briefing." (usually) ?She usually attends the Monday briefing.
"He ___ late for the nine AM meeting." (is / rarely) ?He is rarely late for the nine AM meeting.
"We ___ prioritize client calls over internal tasks." (often) ?We often prioritize client calls over internal tasks.
"I ___ skip my morning routine." (never) ?I never skip my morning routine.
"They ___ busy on Monday mornings." (are / always) ?They are always busy on Monday mornings.
Practice

Reorder: Make Sentences

schedule / I / my / usually / check / morning / in the ?"I usually check my schedule in the morning."
never / she / meetings / skips / Monday ?"She never skips Monday meetings."
team / I / often / my / brief / lunch / after ?"I often brief my team after lunch."
always / on time / is / he ?"He is always on time."
Chapter 4: Getting There

A Day in
Natalie's Life

Your Monday routine in real professional context

Artifact

Natalie's Monday Calendar

Monday -- Natalie Viegas
6:30 AMCommute from Vila Madalena
7:15 AMCheck schedule + review urgent emails
9:00 AMLeadership meeting
10:00 AMBrief team on pipeline + weekly priorities
11:00 AMClient meeting (Ministry of Justice)
12:30 PMLunch
1:30 PMFollow up with stakeholders
3:00 PMCatch up with sales team
5:00 PMReview contracts + prioritize emails
6:00 PMWrap up reports + commute home
Dialogue

Monday Morning with David

D
Good morning, Natalie. How was your commute?
N
Hi David! It was fine. I usually take about forty minutes from Vila Madalena.
D
What is on your schedule today?
N
I need to review the contract for the Ministry of Justice first. Then I attend a client meeting at eleven.
D
Do you always have meetings in the morning?
N
Usually, yes. I also need to brief the team on the pipeline and follow up with two stakeholders before the deadline on Friday.
D
That sounds like a busy day. Do you ever catch up with the team after lunch?
N
Sometimes. I usually wrap up my reports in the afternoon and then prioritize emails before I leave.
Comprehension

About David

1. What does David ask about Natalie's morning?
He asks about her commute and her schedule for the day.
2. Does David think Natalie has a busy day?
Yes, he says "That sounds like a busy day."
3. What does David ask about the afternoon?
He asks if Natalie ever catches up with the team after lunch.
Listening

An Executive's Monday

Listen to an executive describing his typical Monday.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What is the speaker's name and role?
Carlos Mendes, Regional Director at a tech company
2. What does he always do when he arrives?
He checks his schedule and reviews urgent emails
3. What does he do in the afternoon?
He catches up with his sales team and wraps up pending proposals
Comprehension

What Did You Hear?

1. How does Carlos commute to work?
He commutes from Pinheiros to the office in Faria Lima, about thirty minutes.
2. What does Carlos always prioritize?
He always prioritizes client-facing tasks over internal work.
3. When is the deadline for the quarterly report?
Always the last Friday of the month.
Chapter 5: Practice

More
Practice

Listening, quick fire, spot the error, and oral drilling

Listening

Team Planning

Listen to a team planning their week's schedule.

0:000:00
Speed
1. What does the speaker ask Natalie to do?
Brief the team on the Ministry project status
2. What does David need to do?
Follow up with the procurement office today
Comprehension

Team Planning Review

1. When is the deadline?
Friday. The speaker says "The deadline is firm this time."
2. When does the speaker usually schedule client calls?
Wednesday and Thursday.
3. What does everyone need to attend at 2 PM?
The strategy meeting.
Chapter 5: Practice

Quick Challenge

0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
Your manager asks: "How do you start your Mondays?" Describe your morning routine.
"I always check my schedule and review my emails first. Then I attend the leadership meeting at nine."
Detective

Spot the Error

"She always check her emails in the morning."
"She always checks her emails in the morning." (third person -s)
"I usually am commuting by car."
"I usually commute by car." (routine = Present Simple)
"He attend never the Monday meeting."
"He never attends the Monday meeting." (adverb before verb + third person -s)
"I am always review the reports."
"I always review the reports." (Present Simple for routines)
0 / 4 errors found
Speaking

Oral Drilling

1. Say that you always check your schedule in the morning.
"I always check my schedule in the morning."
2. Say that you usually attend the 9 AM meeting.
"I usually attend the nine AM meeting."
3. Say that you need to follow up with the client.
"I need to follow up with the client before the deadline."
4. Say that you never leave without wrapping up your reports.
"I never leave without wrapping up my reports."
5. Say that you often prioritize client meetings.
"I often prioritize client meetings over internal tasks."
6. Say that you rarely miss the Monday meeting.
"I rarely miss the Monday meeting."
Practice

Build the Sentence

always / review / I / before / contracts / calls / client ?"I always review contracts before client calls."
catches up / she / usually / team / with / her / morning / in the ?"She usually catches up with her team in the morning."
wrap up / we / reports / our / never / without / leave ?"We never leave without wrapping up our reports."
often / he / briefs / priorities / on / his team / weekly ?"He often briefs his team on weekly priorities."
Chapter 6: Your Turn

From Guided
to Free

Show what you have learned today

Chapter 6: Your Turn

Role-Play: Describe Your Monday

Your colleague David asks: "What does your typical Monday look like?" Describe your full day from commute to wrapping up, using at least 6 vocabulary words and 3 adverbs of frequency.
schedulecommuteattendbriefreviewalwaysusuallynever
Role-Play

Role-Play: Plan a Meeting

A new colleague asks you to help plan a meeting schedule for the week. Discuss: which meetings to prioritize, deadlines to watch, and when to catch up with the team.
prioritizedeadlinecatch up
Free Practice

Role-Play: Present Your Week

Your VP asks: "Walk me through your ideal work week." Present how you organize your schedule, your routines, and your priorities. No keywords, no script. Just talk!

Use everything you learned today: routines, frequency adverbs, and daily operations vocabulary.

Reflection

Record Yourself

Describe your typical Monday at Microsoft in one minute. Include: your commute, your morning routine, the meetings you attend, and how you wrap up your day. Use at least 3 adverbs of frequency.

Click to start recording. Click again to stop.

Chapter 7: Wrap-Up

Almost Done!

Delayed error correction, survival phrases, and self-assessment

Error Correction

Let's Polish

Share 3-4 errors you noticed during the class. Focus on adverb placement and Present Simple.

What you said What to say instead

This is a safe space for learning. Every mistake is a step forward.

Survival Card

5 Key Phrases

1
"I always check my schedule first thing in the morning."
2
"I need to follow up with the client by Friday."
3
"Let me brief the team on the pipeline status."
4
"I usually catch up with my team at nine AM."
5
"The deadline for this proposal is next Wednesday."
Self-Assessment

What I Learned

I can describe my Monday routine in English.
I know 10 daily operations vocabulary words.
I can use adverbs of frequency correctly (always, usually, often, sometimes, rarely, never).
I know where to place the adverb (before main verb, after BE).
I can talk about my schedule, deadlines, and follow-ups.
I can use phrasal verbs: follow up, catch up, wrap up.
I can describe my commute and daily operations.
I feel more confident describing my work routine in English.
Lesson Complete

Day 2 -- Complete.

Next lesson: Career Story -- From Rio to São Paulo

Lesson 3 -- Business English

Career Story
From Rio to São Paulo

B1+ -- 60 minutes -- Alumni by Better

Chapter 1: Warm-Up

Do You Remember
These Words?

Schedule
Commute
Attend
Deadline
Prioritize
Chapter 1: Warm-Up

Your Career Journey

"Where did you work before Microsoft?"
"Why did you move from Rio to São Paulo?"
"What was the biggest change when you started at Microsoft?"
Chapter 2

Career
Vocabulary

10 words to tell your professional story

Discover the Words

to move to a new place for work
Relocate
to move to a new place, especially for work
"I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago."
to change from one job or situation to another
Transition
to change from one job, role, or situation to another
"I transitioned from a different industry into tech sales."
to try to achieve a goal or career path
Pursue
to try to achieve a goal or follow a career path
"I pursued a career in enterprise sales."
to get something valuable over time
Gain
to get or acquire something valuable over time
"I gained valuable experience with public institutions."
0 / 4 words revealed

More Career Words

to succeed in doing something important
Accomplish
to succeed in doing something important
"We accomplished our revenue target ahead of schedule."
a difficult task that tests your abilities
Challenge
a difficult task or situation that tests your abilities
"The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city."
a chance to do something new or better
Opportunity
a chance to do something new or better
"Microsoft gave me the opportunity to grow."
coming before in time or order
Previous
coming before in time or order
"In my previous role, I managed private sector accounts."
0 / 4 words revealed

Final Words

something important done successfully
Achievement
something important that you have done successfully
"My greatest achievement was closing a huge deal."
an important event or turning point
Milestone
an important event or turning point in development
"Moving to São Paulo was a major milestone."
0 / 2 words revealed

Say It Out Loud

1. "I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago." ♫
2. "I transitioned from a different industry into technology sales." ♫
3. "I pursued a career in enterprise sales." ♫
4. "I gained valuable experience working with public institutions." ♫
5. "We accomplished our revenue target ahead of schedule." ♫
6. "Moving to São Paulo was a major milestone in my career." ♫
Chapter 3

The Code

Past Simple -- Regular and Irregular

Look at the Pattern

"I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago."
"I transitioned into technology sales last year."
"Microsoft gave me the opportunity in 2022."
"When I was in Rio, I worked in a different industry."

What do the purple words have in common?

Reveal the Rule

TypeStructure
Regular (+)verb + -ed
relocated, transitioned, pursued
Irregular (+)special past form
gave, was, went, came, took, made, got, left
Negative (-)did not + base verb
"I did not expect to grow so fast."
Question (?)Did + subject + base verb?
"Did you work in Rio before?"

Common Mistake

"I relocate to SP four years ago."
"I relocated to SP four years ago."

Time expressions like "ago", "last year", "in 2022" require Past Simple.

Complete with Past Simple

"She ___pursued a career in tech sales last year."
"I ___gained valuable experience before joining Microsoft."
"The team ___accomplished all targets in Q1."

More Practice

"Microsoft ___gave me the opportunity to grow."
"I did not ___expect to grow so fast."
"When did you ___transition to Microsoft?"
Chapter 4

In Context

Career stories come to life

Natalie's Career Timeline

BEFORE 2022
Worked in a different industry in Rio de Janeiro
2022
Relocated from Rio to São Paulo
2022
Transitioned into technology sales at Microsoft
2023-2025
Gained experience with public sector clients
LAST YEAR
Accomplished revenue target, closed a major deal

At the Onboarding Event

Marco meets Natalie at a Microsoft onboarding event.

M
Hey Natalie! I am Marco from the Azure team. I just started this week. Where were you before Microsoft?
N
Hi Marco! Welcome! Before Microsoft, I worked in a completely different industry. I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago.
M
That is a big move! What made you transition into tech?
N
I pursued a career in enterprise sales because I wanted to work with bigger clients. Microsoft gave me that opportunity.
M
What was the biggest challenge when you first arrived?
N
The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city and a new industry at the same time. But I gained so much experience.
M
It sounds like you accomplished a lot. What is your proudest achievement here?
N
My greatest achievement last year was closing a multi-million-dollar deal with the Ministry of Justice. That was a real milestone for me.

Did You Understand?

1. Where does Marco work?
Marco works on the Azure team at Microsoft.
2. When did Natalie relocate to São Paulo?
She relocated four years ago.
3. What was Natalie's biggest challenge?
Adapting to a new city and a new industry at the same time.

Listening Challenge

Ricardo Almeida -- VP of Sales -- Career Story

0:000:00
Speed
1. Where did Ricardo start his career?
He started in a small consulting firm in Porto Alegre.
2. What was his biggest achievement?
Building a sales team from scratch that generated over fifty million dollars in revenue.
3. What was his biggest challenge?
Leaving a stable career to pursue something new.

Transcript -- Ricardo's Story

"My name is Ricardo Almeida. I am the Vice President of Sales at a global tech company based in São Paulo. Let me tell you about my career journey. I started in a small consulting firm in Porto Alegre over fifteen years ago. I relocated to São Paulo in 2015 because I wanted to pursue a career in enterprise technology. The transition was not easy. I had to learn a completely new industry. But I gained incredible experience working with Fortune 500 clients. My biggest achievement was building a sales team from scratch that generated over fifty million dollars in revenue. That was a major milestone. Before that, I worked in financial services for seven years. The biggest challenge was leaving a stable career to pursue something new. But I have no regrets. Every move I made led me to where I am today."
Chapter 5

Practice

Test your Past Simple skills

Listening Two

Camila Santos -- Team-Building Event

0:000:00
Speed
1. What did Camila study at university?
She studied marketing.
2. Where did she work before the startup?
She worked at an advertising agency in Belo Horizonte.
3. What was the biggest milestone in her career?
Moving to Microsoft.
0 / 6
Question 1 of 6
A colleague asks: "Where did you work before Microsoft?" Answer in Past Simple.
"In my previous role, I worked in a different industry in Rio de Janeiro."

Spot the Error

"I relocate to São Paulo four years ago."
"I relocated to São Paulo four years ago."
"She didn't gained any experience in sales."
"She didn't gain any experience in sales."
"When you transitioned to Microsoft?"
"When did you transition to Microsoft?"
"He accomplish his target last quarter."
"He accomplished his target last quarter."
0 / 4 errors found

Say These Sentences

1. "I relocated and transitioned into a new career." ♫
2. "My previous role gave me great experience." ♫
3. "That achievement was a major milestone." ♫
4. "I pursued this opportunity because I wanted to grow." ♫
5. "The biggest challenge was adapting to everything at once." ♫
6. "I gained valuable experience and accomplished my targets." ♫

Build Your Sentences

SUBJECT
I
She
We
The team
PAST VERB
relocated
gained
accomplished
pursued
TIME
four years ago
last year
in 2022
before that
"I ___relocated to São Paulo four years ago."
"She ___gained valuable experience last year."
"We ___accomplished our targets in 2022."
Moving Forward

From Practice
to Production

Show what you learned

Chapter 6

Your Turn

From guided to free -- show what you learned

Guided Role-Play

A colleague at a Microsoft networking event asks: "Tell me about your career journey. How did you get to where you are today?"
relocated previous gained challenge opportunity

Semi-Free Role-Play

Share your career story at a networking event. Complete each prompt:
"Before Microsoft, I..."
"I relocated because..."
"The biggest challenge was..."
"My proudest achievement..."

Free Production

Present your career timeline to your VP. No script, no prompts. 2 minutes. Tell your complete career story from Rio to São Paulo.

How Did That Feel?

"Which vocabulary words were the easiest to use?"
"Which time expressions did you use the most?"
"Do you feel more confident talking about your career?"
Chapter 7

Wrap-Up

Review, remember, celebrate

Let's Fine-Tune

Common errors from today's lesson

"I relocate to São Paulo four years ago." ♫
"I relocated to São Paulo four years ago."
"She didn't gained any experience in sales." ♫
"She didn't gain any experience in sales."
"When you transitioned to Microsoft?" ♫
"When did you transition to Microsoft?"
"He accomplish his target last quarter." ♫
"He accomplished his target last quarter."

Survival Card

1I relocated from Rio to São Paulo four years ago.
2In my previous role, I worked in a completely different industry.
3I gained valuable experience working with government clients.
4The biggest challenge was adapting to a new city and a new job.
5Microsoft gave me the opportunity to grow as a professional.

What I Learned

I can tell my career story in Past Simple.
I know 10 career vocabulary words.
I can use time expressions correctly (ago, last year, before that).
I can form Past Simple questions (Did you...? When did you...?).
I can describe my achievements and milestones.
I can explain why I relocated and changed careers.
I can understand someone else's career story in English.
I feel more confident talking about my professional journey.
Lesson Complete

Day 3 -- Complete.

Next lesson: What Are You Working On? -- Current Projects

Lesson 4

What Are You Working On?

Current Projects & Accomplishments

Let's Catch Up

"What projects are you working on this week?"
"Have you finished anything important recently?"
"What is the biggest project on your desk right now?"

Quick Check

"What is your team doing this month?"
"How many projects have you completed this year?"
"Are you working on anything new right now?"
Chapter 2

Building Blocks

Project Management Vocabulary

Project Vocabulary 1/3

0 / 4 words revealed

A formal document presenting a plan for approval

Proposal

a formal plan submitted for approval

"We submitted a proposal to the Ministry of Justice."

The range or extent of a project's objectives

Scope

the range of work in a project

"The scope includes cloud migration and data security."

A specific output that must be completed by a deadline

Deliverable

a result that must be completed

"The first deliverable is due at the end of this sprint."

To set aside money, time, or resources for a purpose

Allocate

to assign resources for a purpose

"I have allocated the budget for next quarter."

Project Vocabulary 2/3

0 / 4 words revealed

To make a process more efficient by removing unnecessary steps

Streamline

to make more efficient

"We need to streamline the procurement process."

To introduce or launch something gradually

Roll out

to launch gradually

"We are rolling out the new system this month."

To evaluate the quality, importance, or progress of something

Assess

to evaluate or judge

"I am assessing the risks before the next sprint."

A short, focused period of intensive work

Sprint

a short work cycle

"The team has completed three sprints so far."

Project Vocabulary 3/3

0 / 2 words revealed

To follow and monitor the progress of something

Track

to monitor progress

"I track all deliverables in our dashboard."

Designed to grow or expand without losing quality

Scalable

able to grow without losing quality

"The system needs to be scalable for other agencies."

Use the Words

1. "We submitted a proposal to the Ministry of Justice." ♫
2. "The scope includes cloud migration and data security." ♫
3. "I am rolling out the new case management system." ♫
4. "I have allocated the budget for next quarter." ♫
5. "We need to streamline the procurement process." ♫
6. "The system is scalable for other agencies." ♫
Chapter 3

The Code

Present Continuous + Present Perfect

Look at the Pattern

"I am working on a cloud migration project right now."
"The team is rolling out the first module this month."
"We have completed three sprints so far."
"I have allocated the budget already."

What do the purple words have in common?

Reveal the Rule

Present Continuous (NOW)
Affirmativeam/is/are + verb-ing
"I am working on a proposal."
Negativeam/is/are + not + verb-ing
"We are not tracking that yet."
QuestionAm/Is/Are + subject + verb-ing?
"Are you rolling out the system?"
Present Perfect (RESULTS)
Affirmativehave/has + past participle
"I have allocated the budget."
Negativehave/has + not + past participle
"We have not finished the review."
QuestionHave/Has + subject + past participle?
"Have you completed the sprint?"

Common Mistakes

"I am work on a new proposal."
"I am working on a new proposal."
"We have complete three sprints."
"We have completed three sprints."

Complete with the Correct Form

"She ___is rolling out the new system this month." (roll out — NOW)
"I ___have allocated the budget already." (allocate — RESULT)
"We ___are tracking the deliverables right now." (track — NOW)

More Practice

"___Have you ___finished the compliance review?" (question — RESULT)
"We ___have not assessed the risks yet." (negative — RESULT)
"___Is the team ___working on the security module?" (question — NOW)

Continuous or Perfect?

"I am assessing the risks this week." → ___Present Continuous (happening now)
"We have delivered 12 out of 15 features." → ___Present Perfect (result so far)
"The team is streamlining the process." → ___Present Continuous (in progress now)
"She has not finished the proposal yet." → ___Present Perfect (unfinished result)
Chapter 4

In the Field

Real Project Updates

Project Dashboard

Ministry of Justice
Digital Transformation Project
IN PROGRESS
3/5
Sprints Completed
12/15
Deliverables Done
Project Lead: Natalie Viegas
Budget Status: Allocated for Q3
Current Focus: Rolling out case management module
Next Step: Assessing requirements for 2 new agencies

Project Update Call

Natalie talks to VP Carlos Rodriguez about the Ministry project.

CR
Carlos Rodriguez
"Good morning, Natalie. Can you give me a quick update on the Ministry of Justice project?"
NV
Natalie
"Of course, Carlos. We are making great progress. We have completed three sprints so far."
CR
Carlos Rodriguez
"That is excellent. What are you working on right now?"
NV
Natalie
"I am currently rolling out the first module. The team is tracking all the deliverables in our project dashboard."
CR
Carlos Rodriguez
"Have you allocated the budget for next quarter?"
NV
Natalie
"Yes, I have. I have also streamlined the approval process. It is now thirty percent faster."
CR
Carlos Rodriguez
"Impressive. Is the system scalable for other agencies?"
NV
Natalie
"Absolutely. We have designed it to be scalable from the start. Two other agencies have already expressed interest."

Dialogue Comprehension

1. How many sprints have they completed?

They have completed three sprints so far.

2. What is Natalie currently rolling out?

She is rolling out the first module of the case management system.

3. What has Natalie streamlined?

She has streamlined the approval process, making it 30% faster.

Listening 1 -- Team Standup

0:00 / 0:00

1. What module is the team currently working on?

They are working on the security module.

2. Has the scope changed since the proposal was approved?

No, the scope has not changed.

3. What does Natalie need to allocate more of?

She needs to allocate more resources to the compliance review.

Listening 2 -- Project Briefing

0:00 / 0:00

1. How many deliverables are they tracking?

They are tracking fifteen deliverables, twelve already complete.

2. What has Natalie streamlined?

She has streamlined the procurement process.

3. How many additional agencies have shown interest?

Two additional agencies have expressed interest.

Spot the Error

"I am work on a new proposal right now." ♫
"I am working on a new proposal right now."
"We have complete three sprints so far." ♫
"We have completed three sprints so far."
"She is allocate the budget this week." ♫
"She is allocating the budget this week."
"They has already rolled out the system." ♫
"They have already rolled out the system."
Chapter 5

Mission Training

Quick Fire Practice

Quick Fire 1/4

Your VP asks you in a meeting:
"What are you working on this week?"

Example: "I am currently rolling out the first module of the case management system. I am also assessing the risks for the next sprint."

Quick Fire 2/4

A colleague asks:
"What have you accomplished on the project so far?"

Example: "We have completed three sprints so far. I have allocated the budget for Q3 and streamlined the approval process."

Quick Fire 3/4

At a global sync, someone asks:
"Can you give us a project update?"

Example: "We have delivered twelve out of fifteen features. We are currently rolling out the case management module, and I am tracking the remaining deliverables."

Quick Fire 4/4

A potential client from another agency asks:
"Is your system ready for other government agencies?"

Example: "Yes, we have designed it to be scalable from the start. Two other agencies have already expressed interest. We are assessing their requirements this month."

Chapter 6

Your Mission

Project Briefing Role-Play

Role-Play: Guided

Scenario: Your VP asks for a project update in a weekly meeting. Use the keywords below.

sprint deliverable rolling out allocated tracking

Role-Play: Semi-Free

Scenario: A colleague from the Asia Pacific team asks about your project. Describe what you are doing now AND what you have accomplished.

scalable scope proposal

Role-Play: Free

Scenario: You are presenting a full project briefing to the global leadership team. No prompts, no keywords. 2 minutes. Give a complete update on your Ministry of Justice project.

How Did That Feel?

"Was it easier to describe what you ARE DOING or what you HAVE DONE?"
"Which project vocabulary words came most naturally?"
"Do you feel more confident giving project updates in English?"
Chapter 7

Wrap-Up

Review, remember, celebrate

Let's Fine-Tune

Common errors from today's lesson

"I am work on a new proposal right now." ♫
"I am working on a new proposal right now."
"We have complete three sprints so far." ♫
"We have completed three sprints so far."
"She is allocate the budget this week." ♫
"She is allocating the budget this week."
"They has already rolled out the system." ♫
"They have already rolled out the system."

Survival Card

1I am currently working on a major project for the Ministry of Justice.
2We have completed three sprints so far.
3I have allocated the budget for next quarter.
4The team is tracking all the deliverables.
5The system has been designed to be scalable.

What I Learned

I can describe my current projects using Present Continuous.
I can talk about results using Present Perfect.
I know 10 project management vocabulary words.
I can give a project update to my VP in English.
I understand the difference between "am working" and "have worked."
I can use signal words (now, currently, so far, already, yet).
I can describe project deliverables and track progress.
I feel more confident presenting a project briefing in English.
Lesson Complete

Day 4 -- Complete.

Next lesson: The Art of Meetings -- Leading with Confidence

T
LESSON 1
